methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the instance.  See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Override in subclasses to define the computation.

methods.name: __call__
methods.summary: Forward to the underlying callable (see class docstring).

methods.name: parameters
methods.summary: Yield all Parameters in this module (and children if recurse=True).

methods.name: named_parameters
methods.signature: named_parameters(prefix: str = '', recurse: bool = True, remove_duplicate: bool = True)
methods.summary: Yield `(qualified_name, Parameter)` pairs from this module's tree.

parameters.name: prefix
parameters.description:
String prepended to every yielded name — used internally by
the recursive walk to namespace child modules.  Default
`""`.

parameters.name: recurse
parameters.description:
When `True` (default), descend into submodules.  When
`False`, yield only this module's directly-attached
parameters.

parameters.name: remove_duplicate
parameters.description:
When `True` (default), each unique `Parameter`
object is yielded only once even if referenced by multiple
attributes — matches the reference framework's contract.

methods.name: buffers
methods.summary: Yield all buffer tensors.

methods.name: named_buffers
methods.signature: named_buffers(prefix: str = '', recurse: bool = True, remove_duplicate: bool = True)
methods.summary: Yield (name, buffer) pairs.

methods.name: modules
methods.summary: Yield this module and all submodules (depth-first).

methods.name: named_modules
methods.signature: named_modules(memo: set[int] | None = None, prefix: str = '', remove_duplicate: bool = True)
methods.summary: Yield (name, module) pairs.

methods.name: children
methods.summary: Yield direct child modules.

methods.name: named_children
methods.summary: Yield (name, child_module) pairs.

methods.name: get_submodule
methods.summary: Return submodule at dotted path, e.g. 'encoder.layer.0'.

methods.name: get_parameter
methods.summary: Return parameter at dotted path, e.g. 'fc.weight'.

methods.name: get_buffer
methods.summary: Return buffer at dotted path, e.g. 'bn.running_mean'.

methods.name: register_parameter
methods.summary: Register a Parameter under the given name.

methods.name: register_buffer
methods.signature: register_buffer(name: str, tensor: Tensor | None, persistent: bool = True)
methods.summary: Register a buffer tensor. Non-persistent buffers are excluded from state_dict.

methods.name: add_module
methods.summary: Add a child module.

methods.name: register_module
methods.summary: Alias for add_module.

methods.name: train
methods.summary: Set this module and all children to training mode.

methods.name: eval
methods.summary: Set this module and all children to evaluation mode.

methods.name: to
methods.summary: Move/cast all parameters and buffers, preserving Parameter object identity.
methods.extended:
Floating-point dtype casts (`.float()`, `.double()`,
`.half()`, `.bfloat16()`) skip integer buffers — e.g.
`BatchNorm.num_batches_tracked` stays int64 — matching the
reference framework so checkpoint round-trips don't quietly
widen / narrow the counter type.  Device moves still apply to
every tensor.

methods.name: metal
methods.summary: Move all parameters and buffers to Apple Metal GPU.

methods.name: cpu
methods.summary: Move all parameters and buffers to CPU.

methods.name: half
methods.summary: Cast all parameters and buffers to float16.

methods.name: float
methods.summary: Cast all parameters and buffers to float32.

methods.name: double
methods.summary: Cast all parameters and buffers to float64.

methods.name: bfloat16
methods.summary: Cast all parameters and buffers to bfloat16.

methods.name: type
methods.summary: Cast all parameters and buffers to *dst_type*.
methods.extended:
*dst_type* may be a `lucid.dtype`, a Python type (`float`,
`int`), or a string (`"float32"`, `"float16"`, etc.).
Delegates to `to`, which handles the conversion.

methods.name: apply
methods.summary: Apply fn recursively to every submodule (including self).

methods.name: zero_grad
methods.summary: Zero gradients of all parameters.

methods.name: requires_grad_
methods.summary: Set requires_grad for all parameters.

methods.name: share_memory
methods.summary: No-op on Apple Silicon (unified memory is always shared).

methods.name: compile
methods.summary: No-op compatibility stub.
methods.extended:
External codepaths often call `model.compile()` to opt into JIT
acceleration; Lucid has no such layer, so this returns `self`
unchanged rather than crashing the caller.  Any positional or
keyword arguments are accepted and ignored.

methods.name: to_empty
methods.signature: to_empty(device: object = None, recurse: bool = True)
methods.summary: Move parameters and buffers to `device` without copying data.
methods.extended:
The reference framework uses `to_empty` to materialise a
model originally constructed on the meta device.  Lucid has no
meta device, so this method exists for API parity and
delegates to the standard `to` when a device is supplied.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description:
Target placement (string, `device`, or engine enum).
When `None` (default) the call is a no-op returning
`self`.

parameters.name: recurse
parameters.description:
Honoured by the underlying `to` call.  Default
`True` — every child module is moved as well.

returns.description: The same module, parameters / buffers now on `device`.

methods.name: get_extra_state
methods.summary: Return extra state to include in state_dict. Override in subclasses.

methods.name: set_extra_state
methods.summary: Restore extra state loaded from state_dict. Override in subclasses.

methods.name: state_dict
methods.signature: state_dict(destination: dict[str, Tensor] | None = None, prefix: str = '', keep_vars: bool = False)
methods.summary: Return an ordered dict mapping parameter / buffer names to tensors.
methods.extended:
Includes every learnable parameter, every persistent buffer
(`persistent=True` at register time), and every nested
submodule's contribution under a dotted-path prefix.

The returned `OrderedDict` carries a `_metadata`
attribute mapping `module_path → {"version": int}` for every
module that defines a `_version` class attribute.
`lucid.save` preserves this attribute across disk
round-trips so version-aware `_load_from_state_dict` hooks
can migrate older checkpoints.

parameters.name: destination
parameters.description:
When supplied, results are written into this dict instead
of a fresh one.  Useful when composing state dicts from
multiple modules.  Default `None`.

parameters.name: prefix
parameters.description:
String prepended to every key — used internally by the
recursive walk to namespace child modules.  Default `""`.

parameters.name: keep_vars
parameters.description:
When `True` keep tensors attached to autograd (return
them as-is); when `False` (default) detach them so the
state dict is safe to serialise / cross threads.

returns.description:
OrderedDict mapping qualified parameter / buffer paths to
their tensor values.

methods.name: load_state_dict
methods.signature: load_state_dict(state_dict: dict[str, Tensor], strict: bool = True, assign: bool = False)
methods.summary: Load parameters from a state_dict.
methods.extended:
Calls each module's `_load_from_state_dict` recursively.
Returns `_IncompatibleKeys(missing_keys, unexpected_keys)` on success.
Raises `RuntimeError` if `strict=True` and any keys are missing
or unexpected, or if any error_msgs accumulated during loading.

parameters.name: state_dict
parameters.description: A mapping from parameter/buffer names to tensors.

parameters.name: strict
parameters.description:
If `True` (default) require an exact key match; raise on any
missing or unexpected keys.

parameters.name: assign
parameters.description:
If `True` replace each parameter/buffer object with the
loaded tensor directly (allows shape/dtype changes).  If
`False` (default) copy data into the existing parameter
preserving its dtype and device.

methods.name: register_load_state_dict_pre_hook
methods.summary: Register a pre-hook called when this module loads state_dict.
methods.extended:
Hook signature:
    hook(module, state_dict, prefix, local_metadata, strict,
         missing_keys, unexpected_keys, error_msgs) -> None

The hook may mutate state_dict/missing/unexpected/error_msgs.

methods.name: register_load_state_dict_post_hook
methods.summary: Register a post-hook called after this module loads state_dict.

methods.name: register_forward_pre_hook
methods.signature: register_forward_pre_hook(hook: _ForwardPreHook, prepend: bool = False, with_kwargs: bool = False)
methods.summary: Register a hook called before forward().

methods.name: register_forward_hook
methods.signature: register_forward_hook(hook: _ForwardHook, prepend: bool = False, with_kwargs: bool = False, always_call: bool = False)
methods.summary: Register a hook called after forward().

methods.name: register_full_backward_pre_hook
methods.summary: Register a hook to be called before backward hooks.

methods.name: register_full_backward_hook
methods.summary: Register a backward hook. Returns a RemovableHandle.

methods.name: register_backward_hook
methods.summary: Deprecated alias for register_full_backward_hook.

methods.name: __repr__
methods.summary: Return a developer-facing string representation of the instance.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Override to add extra repr info (e.g. Linear shows in_features, etc.).

members.summary: Base class for all neural network modules.
members.extended:
Every custom model should subclass this and implement `forward`.
Submodules assigned as attributes are tracked automatically.
examples:
>>> class MLP(nn.Module):
...     def __init__(self):
...         super().__init__()
...         self.fc1 = nn.Linear(10, 20)
...         self.fc2 = nn.Linear(20, 1)
...
...     def forward(self, x):
...         return self.fc2(lucid.relu(self.fc1(x)))
...
>>> model = MLP()
>>> model(lucid.randn(4, 10)).shape
(4, 1)
notes:
**Attribute routing**: Setting an attribute follows this priority order:

1. If the value is a `lucid.nn.Parameter` → stored in `_parameters`.
2. If the value is a `Module` → stored in `_modules`.
3. Otherwise → plain Python attribute.

To register a non-parameter tensor (e.g. a running mean), call
`register_buffer` explicitly.

members.name: Parameter
members.signature: Parameter(data: Tensor | list[object] | None = None, requires_grad: bool = True)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(data: Tensor | list[object] | None = None, requires_grad: bool = True)
methods.summary: Initialise the instance.  See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: __repr__
methods.summary: Return a developer-facing string representation of the instance.

members.summary: A `Tensor` subclass that `Module` recognises as a learnable parameter.
members.extended:
When an instance is bound as an attribute of a `Module`, the module's
`__setattr__` hook detects the `_is_parameter` marker and routes it
into the module's `_parameters` registry instead of treating it as a
plain Tensor attribute.  This is what makes `Module.parameters()` and
`Module.named_parameters()` discover the tensor automatically — no
manual registration call is needed.

By default the underlying tensor has `requires_grad=True`, so it
participates in the autograd graph from the moment of construction.
Pass `requires_grad=False` for "buffer-like" learnables (e.g.
BatchNorm's running mean) that should still be tracked under
`state_dict` but shouldn't accumulate gradients.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description:
Initial values.  `None` (default) yields a zero-sized
placeholder F32/CPU parameter, useful when the actual shape is
deferred until first `forward` (lazy modules).  A `Tensor`
clones its storage so the new parameter is independent of the
source.  A list / nested-list is converted via `_to_impl`.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description:
Whether to record gradient flow through this parameter
(default: `True`).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn import Parameter, Module
>>> class Affine(Module):
...     def __init__(self):
...         super().__init__()
...         self.w = Parameter(lucid.randn(3))  # auto-registered
...         self.b = Parameter(lucid.zeros(3))
>>> m = Affine()
>>> list(m.named_parameters())   # both surface here automatically
[('w', Parameter ...), ('b', Parameter ...)]
notes:
Subclassing `Tensor` (rather than wrapping one) keeps every tensor
operation usable on a `Parameter` without unwrap boilerplate — e.g.
`p + 1` returns a `Tensor`, while `p.data` and `p.grad` still
work.  The `_is_parameter` class attribute is the sole behavioural
difference visible to `Module`.

attributes.name: _is_parameter
attributes.description:
Class-level marker (always `True`) consumed by `Module`'s
attribute-binding logic.

members.name: RemovableHandle
members.signature: RemovableHandle(hooks: dict[int, object], key: int, extra_sets: tuple[set[int], ...] = ())

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(hooks: dict[int, object], key: int, extra_sets: tuple[set[int], ...] = ())
methods.summary: Initialise the handle.  See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: remove
methods.summary: Deregister the hook this handle refers to.
methods.extended:
Pops the hook entry from the owning registry dict and discards the
key from any auxiliary sets.  Idempotent — calling `remove` a
second time is a no-op (`pop(..., None)`).

methods.name: __enter__
methods.summary: Enter the `with` block; return `self` for binding via `as`.

methods.name: __exit__
methods.summary: Exit the `with` block, auto-removing the hook registration.

members.summary: Lightweight handle returned by every `register_*_hook` call.
members.extended:
Holds a reference to the dict (or set) that owns the registered hook
plus the unique key under which it lives.  Calling `remove`
deregisters the hook — subsequent forward / backward / load-state-dict
passes will skip it.  Also supports the context manager protocol so
hooks can be scoped to a `with` block.

parameters.name: hooks
parameters.description:
The hook registry dict (e.g. `_GLOBAL_FORWARD_HOOKS`) whose
entry should be removed when `remove` is called.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: Unique hook identifier returned by `_next_hook_id()`.

parameters.name: extra_sets
parameters.description:
Auxiliary sets (e.g. for "always-called" hooks) from which the
same key should also be discarded on removal.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn import hooks
>>> def my_hook(mod, inputs):
...     print(f'about to call {type(mod).__name__}')
>>> h = hooks.register_module_forward_pre_hook(my_hook)
>>> # ... forward passes print the message ...
>>> h.remove()                    # explicit deregistration
>>>
>>> # Or via context manager for scoped activation:
>>> with hooks.register_module_forward_pre_hook(my_hook):
...     model(x)                  # hook fires here
>>> # hook auto-removed on context exit
notes:
The handle does NOT own the hook callable — it only knows where to
delete the registration.  Holding a handle therefore prevents nothing
on its own; once `remove` is called the hook stops firing
regardless of any references the caller still holds.

members.name: register_module_full_backward_hook
members.signature: register_module_full_backward_hook(hook: Callable[..., object])
members.summary: Register a global post-hook fired after a module's backward pass.
members.extended:
Invoked once Lucid's autograd engine has finished accumulating
gradients for a Module.  The hook receives the module along with the
input-gradients (gradients flowing back into the module's inputs) and
the output-gradients (gradients that came from above).  Mutating these
in place propagates to subsequent backward computation; returning a
new tuple of input-gradients replaces them.

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description: Signature: `hook(module, grad_input, grad_output) -> None | tuple`.

returns.description: Handle for later deregistration.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.hooks import register_module_full_backward_hook
>>> def log_grad_norm(mod, grad_input, grad_output):
...     for i, g in enumerate(grad_input):
...         if g is not None:
...             print(f'{type(mod).__name__}.in[{i}].grad_norm = {g.norm().item()}')
>>> handle = register_module_full_backward_hook(log_grad_norm)
notes:
Use the "full" variant whenever a Module has multiple inputs and you
need access to every per-input gradient.  Pairs naturally with
`register_module_full_backward_pre_hook` for modify-then-observe
workflows.

members.name: register_module_full_backward_pre_hook
members.signature: register_module_full_backward_pre_hook(hook: Callable[..., object])
members.summary: Register a global pre-hook fired before any module's backward pass.
members.extended:
Invoked at the moment Lucid's autograd engine is about to compute
gradients for a Module's parameters.  The hook receives the module and
the gradient(s) of the module's output(s); it may return a modified
tuple of grad-outputs to inject custom backward behaviour (e.g.
gradient clipping, sign-SGD-style modifications).

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description:
Signature: `hook(module, grad_output) -> None | tuple`.
Returning a tuple replaces the gradient flowing into the module
before any registered post-hook runs.

returns.description: Handle for later deregistration.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.hooks import register_module_full_backward_pre_hook
>>> def scale_grads(mod, grad_output):
...     return tuple(g * 0.5 for g in grad_output)
>>> h = register_module_full_backward_pre_hook(scale_grads)
notes:
"Full" backward pre-hooks are paired with **full** backward hooks
(`register_module_full_backward_hook`).  Both fire only when
autograd actually visits the module's backward node — modules whose
output gradient is never requested (e.g. frozen branches) are skipped.

members.name: register_module_forward_hook
members.signature: register_module_forward_hook(hook: Callable[..., object], with_kwargs: bool = False, always_call: bool = False)
members.summary: Register a global post-forward hook fired after every `Module.__call__`.
members.extended:
The hook runs **after** `forward` returns and may inspect or replace
the output.  Common uses: activation logging / visualisation, output
transformation (e.g. moving outputs to a different device), or
side-effect bookkeeping.

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description:
The post-hook function.  Signature:

* `hook(module, inputs, output) -> None | new_output`                (default)
* `hook(module, inputs, kwargs, output) -> None | new_output`        (`with_kwargs=True`)

Returning `None` leaves the output unchanged; returning any
other value replaces the forward output.

parameters.name: with_kwargs
parameters.description:
If `True`, the hook also receives the kwargs dict that was
passed to `forward`.

parameters.name: always_call
parameters.description:
If `True`, the hook fires even when `forward` raises an
exception (the `output` argument is then `None`).  Useful for
cleanup-style hooks.

returns.description: Handle for later deregistration.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.hooks import register_module_forward_hook
>>> activations = {}
>>> def capture(mod, inputs, output):
...     activations[type(mod).__name__] = output.detach()
>>> with register_module_forward_hook(capture):
...     _ = model(x)
>>> activations.keys()
dict_keys([...])
notes:
Global forward hooks fire for **every** Module call.  When multiple
hooks are registered they run in registration order and each may see
the previous hook's replacement output — a chain of transforms.

members.name: register_module_forward_pre_hook
members.signature: register_module_forward_pre_hook(hook: Callable[..., object], with_kwargs: bool = False)
members.summary: Register a global pre-forward hook fired on every `Module.__call__`.
members.extended:
The hook is invoked **before** the wrapped module's `forward` method
runs.  It receives the module instance and the positional inputs (and,
optionally, the keyword inputs) and may return a tuple that replaces
the positional inputs for the upcoming forward pass — useful for
automatic input normalisation, logging, or shape validation.

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description:
The pre-hook function.  Signature:

* `hook(module, inputs) -> None | tuple`                (default)
* `hook(module, inputs, kwargs) -> None | tuple`        (`with_kwargs=True`)

Returning `None` leaves the inputs untouched; returning a
non-`None` tuple replaces the positional inputs.

parameters.name: with_kwargs
parameters.description:
If `True`, the hook also receives the kwargs dict and may
replace it (return `(new_inputs, new_kwargs)`).  Default
`False` for compatibility with reference-framework idioms.

returns.description: Handle whose `RemovableHandle.remove` deregisters the hook.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.hooks import register_module_forward_pre_hook
>>> def log_call(mod, inputs):
...     print(f'{type(mod).__name__}({inputs[0].shape})')
>>> handle = register_module_forward_pre_hook(log_call)
>>> # ... forward passes trigger the log ...
>>> handle.remove()
notes:
Global hooks fire for **every** Module instance.  For per-module hooks
use `Module.register_forward_pre_hook` instead.  The order of
hook execution is registration order (FIFO).

members.name: register_module_load_state_dict_pre_hook
members.signature: register_module_load_state_dict_pre_hook(hook: Callable[..., object])
members.summary: Register a global pre-hook fired for every Module during `load_state_dict`.
members.extended:
Invoked **before** a module attempts to copy values from a state-dict
into its own parameters / buffers.  The hook may mutate `state_dict`
in place to rename keys, transform tensors (e.g. cast dtype, adjust
shape after architectural changes), or pre-populate
`missing_keys`/`unexpected_keys` to influence `strict=True`
behaviour.

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description:
Signature:

    hook(module, state_dict, prefix, local_metadata, strict,
         missing_keys, unexpected_keys, error_msgs) -> None

All list / dict arguments may be mutated in place.

returns.description: Handle for later deregistration.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.hooks import register_module_load_state_dict_pre_hook
>>> def rename_keys(mod, sd, prefix, *args):
...     # Rename legacy keys on the fly.
...     for k in list(sd):
...         if k.startswith(prefix + 'fc.'):
...             sd[k.replace('fc.', 'linear.', 1)] = sd.pop(k)
>>> h = register_module_load_state_dict_pre_hook(rename_keys)
>>> # model.load_state_dict(legacy_checkpoint) now succeeds
notes:
Common application: backward-compatible checkpoint loading after
refactors — e.g. rename `"layer.weight"` to `"linear.weight"` to
accommodate a renamed submodule without forcing users to regenerate
checkpoints.

members.name: register_module_load_state_dict_post_hook
members.signature: register_module_load_state_dict_post_hook(hook: Callable[..., object])
members.summary: Register a global post-hook fired after every Module's `load_state_dict`.
members.extended:
Invoked **after** the copy from state-dict to parameters has happened.
The hook receives the module and a NamedTuple-like `incompatible_keys`
object summarising any `missing_keys` / `unexpected_keys` that
occurred — useful for warning the user, clearing those keys to
silence `strict=True` errors, or running follow-up reinitialisation
on any parameter that wasn't loaded.

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description:
Signature: `hook(module, incompatible_keys) -> None`.

`incompatible_keys` has two list attributes / fields:
`missing_keys` and `unexpected_keys`.  Mutating either list
is reflected in the caller's error handling.

returns.description: Handle for later deregistration.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.hooks import register_module_load_state_dict_post_hook
>>> def reinit_missing(mod, incompatible):
...     for key in incompatible.missing_keys:
...         print(f'(post-load) {key} not in checkpoint — keeping init values')
>>> h = register_module_load_state_dict_post_hook(reinit_missing)
notes:
Common application: re-initialise newly-added layers after loading a
legacy checkpoint that doesn't contain entries for them — keeps the
rest of the model from being clobbered while bringing the new
parameters to a sensible starting point.

members.name: Linear
members.signature: Linear(in_features: int, out_features: int, bias: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(in_features: int, out_features: int, bias: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
methods.summary: Initialise the Linear module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: reset_parameters
methods.summary: Initialize weight with Kaiming uniform and bias with uniform fan_in bound.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the linear transformation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(*, \text{in\_features})$.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape $(*, \text{out\_features})$.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Apply a learnable affine transformation to incoming data.
members.extended:
Computes the linear map

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x} \mathbf{W}^{\top} + \mathbf{b}
$$

where $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}} \times d_{\text{in}}}$
is the weight matrix and $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}}}$ is
the optional bias vector.

parameters.name: in_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of each input sample ($d_{\text{in}}$).

parameters.name: out_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of each output sample ($d_{\text{out}}$).

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True` (default) a learnable bias $\mathbf{b}$ is added to the
output.  Set to `False` when a subsequent normalization layer already
absorbs the bias (e.g. `BatchNorm1d`).

parameters.name: device
parameters.description:
Device on which the initial parameters are allocated (`'cpu'` or
`'metal'`).  Defaults to the global default device.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description:
Floating-point dtype for the initial parameters.  Defaults to the global
default dtype (`float32`).

examples:
Basic usage with a 2-D input:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Linear(20, 10)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 20)   # batch of 4, 20 features each
>>> y = m(x)
>>> y.shape
(4, 10)
Higher-dimensional inputs (batch + sequence):
>>> m = nn.Linear(512, 256)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 32, 512)   # (batch, seq_len, d_model)
>>> m(x).shape
(2, 32, 256)
Disable bias for use before a normalization layer:
>>> m_no_bias = nn.Linear(128, 64, bias=False)
>>> m_no_bias.bias is None
True
>>> lucid.randn(8, 128).shape == (8, 128)
True
notes:
- **Input**: $(\ast, d_{\text{in}})$ — any number of leading batch
  dimensions followed by `in_features`.
- **Output**: $(\ast, d_{\text{out}})$ — same leading dimensions,
  last axis replaced by `out_features`.

`Linear` is the most common building block in feed-forward sub-layers
(e.g. the MLP inside a Transformer block uses two `Linear` layers with a
non-linearity in between).  When composing many layers in sequence the
Kaiming initialization ensures that neither the forward activations nor the
backward gradients explode or vanish at the start of training.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable weight matrix of shape `(out_features, in_features)`.
Initialized with **Kaiming uniform** sampling:



$$
\mathbf{W}_{ij} \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left(
    -\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{(1 + a^2)\,d_{\text{in}}}},\;
    \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{(1 + a^2)\,d_{\text{in}}}}
\right)
$$

where $a = \sqrt{5}$ is the default negative-slope parameter.
This keeps gradient variance roughly constant across layers at
initialization — critical for training stability in deep networks.

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
Learnable bias vector of shape `(out_features,)`.  Initialized with
uniform sampling over $\left[-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{\text{in}}}},\; \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{\text{in}}}}\right]$.
`None` when `bias=False`.

members.name: Identity
members.signature: Identity()

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the linear transformation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(*, \text{in\_features})$.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape $(*, \text{out\_features})$.

members.summary: Pass-through layer that returns its input unchanged.
members.extended:
For any input $\mathbf{x}$ the layer computes

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}
$$

with no learnable parameters.
examples:
Use as a drop-in replacement for a disabled component:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> layer = nn.Identity()
>>> x = lucid.randn(3, 64)
>>> y = layer(x)
>>> (y - x).abs().max().item() == 0.0
True
Conditional head in an nn.Sequential-style pipeline:
>>> use_projection = False
>>> proj = nn.Linear(512, 512) if use_projection else nn.Identity()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 512)
>>> proj(x).shape
(1, 512)
notes:
`Identity` is primarily useful as a **structural placeholder** when a
layer slot must be filled but no transformation is desired.  Common
scenarios include:

* **Ablation studies** — swap out a component (e.g. a dropout or
  normalization layer) with `Identity` to isolate its effect without
  refactoring the surrounding `nn.Sequential`.
* **Conditional composition** — build models where a sub-network is
  either a real layer or a no-op depending on a configuration flag:

      head = nn.Linear(256, num_classes) if use_head else nn.Identity()

* **Skip connections** — act as the identity branch in a residual block
  when the channel dimensions already match, avoiding any projection.

- **Input**: any shape $(\ast)$.
- **Output**: identical shape $(\ast)$ — same data, same storage.

members.summary: Apply a bilinear transformation to a pair of input tensors.
members.extended:
For each output unit $k$ the layer computes

$$
y_k = \mathbf{x}_1^{\top} \mathbf{W}_{k,:,:}\, \mathbf{x}_2 + b_k,
\qquad k = 1, \dots, d_{\text{out}}
$$

where $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}} \times d_{\text{in}_1} \times d_{\text{in}_2}}$ is the weight tensor and
$\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}}}$ is the optional bias.

parameters.name: in1_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of the first input ($d_{\text{in}_1}$).

parameters.name: in2_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of the second input ($d_{\text{in}_2}$).

parameters.name: out_features
parameters.description: Number of output units ($d_{\text{out}}$).

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True` (default) add a learnable bias term $\mathbf{b}$.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for initial parameters.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Dtype for initial parameters.

examples:
Scoring query–key compatibility in an attention mechanism:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> attn_score = nn.Bilinear(64, 64, 1)
>>> q = lucid.randn(8, 64)   # queries
>>> k = lucid.randn(8, 64)   # keys
>>> scores = attn_score(q, k)
>>> scores.shape
(8, 1)
Multi-output relation layer with different input spaces:
>>> rel = nn.Bilinear(128, 256, 32)
>>> x1 = lucid.randn(4, 128)
>>> x2 = lucid.randn(4, 256)
>>> rel(x1, x2).shape
(4, 32)
notes:
`Bilinear` captures *multiplicative interactions* between two feature
vectors that a plain `Linear` layer cannot express.  Typical use cases
include:

* **Attention scoring** — compute compatibility between query and key
  vectors using a learned interaction matrix instead of the dot product.
* **Relation networks** — model pair-wise relationships in graph
  neural networks or visual question answering.
* **Similarity scoring** — learn an asymmetric distance metric between
  two embeddings (e.g. in contrastive or metric-learning settings).

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Weight tensor of shape `(out_features, in1_features, in2_features)`.
Each slice `weight[k]` is a matrix that mixes the two input spaces
for the $k$-th output unit.  Initialized with uniform sampling
over $\left[-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{\text{in}_1}}},\; \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{\text{in}_1}}}\right]$.

members.summary: Linear layer whose input dimension is inferred on the first forward call.
members.extended:
`LazyLinear` defers weight allocation until it receives its first input
tensor.  At that point it reads `x.shape[-1]` to determine
`in_features`, allocates and initializes `weight` and `bias`, and
then performs the standard affine transformation

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x} \mathbf{W}^{\top} + \mathbf{b}
$$

All subsequent calls behave identically to `Linear`.

parameters.name: out_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of each output sample ($d_{\text{out}}$).

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True` (default) a learnable bias is added to the output.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which the parameters will be allocated once materialized.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Dtype for the materialized parameters.

examples:
Infer input size from actual data:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.LazyLinear(64)
>>> m.weight is None
True
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 128)
>>> y = m(x)              # triggers materialization
>>> m.in_features
128
>>> y.shape
(4, 64)
Works with arbitrary leading batch dimensions:
>>> m2 = nn.LazyLinear(32)
>>> x2 = lucid.randn(2, 10, 256)
>>> m2(x2).shape
(2, 10, 32)
Restore from a checkpoint without running a forward pass first:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> # Suppose we saved a trained LazyLinear that had in_features=512.
>>> src = nn.Linear(512, 64)
>>> ckpt = src.state_dict()
>>> lazy = nn.LazyLinear(64)
>>> lazy.weight is None
True
>>> lazy.load_state_dict(ckpt)  # materializes to (64, 512) from ckpt shape
>>> lazy.in_features
512
notes:
**When to prefer** `LazyLinear` **over** `Linear`:

* The input width is only known at runtime (e.g. it depends on a
  preceding convolutional feature extractor whose spatial size varies
  with the input image resolution).
* You want to prototype model architectures without tracking every
  intermediate feature dimension by hand.

**State-dict loading** — If `load_state_dict` is called while the
layer is still uninitialized, the implementation reads the saved weight
shape, materializes the parameters to the correct size, and then
proceeds with the standard copy.  This means a serialized
`LazyLinear` checkpoint can be restored even without a forward
pass.

**Important**: once materialized, the layer behaves *exactly* like a
`Linear` with the same `in_features`.  There is no runtime
overhead after the first call.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
`None` before the first forward call.  After materialization,
a `Parameter` of shape `(out_features, in_features)` initialized
with Kaiming uniform.

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
`None` before the first forward call (and permanently `None` when
`bias=False`).  After materialization, a `Parameter` of shape
`(out_features,)` initialized with uniform fan-in bounds.

attributes.name: in_features
attributes.description:
`None` until the layer is materialized.  Afterwards stores the
inferred input dimensionality.

attributes.name: out_features
attributes.description: The output dimensionality supplied at construction time.

methods.summary: Initialise the FusedLinear module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the linear transformation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(*, \text{in\_features})$.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape $(*, \text{out\_features})$.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Linear layer with a kernel-fused non-linear activation.
members.extended:
Computes

$$
\mathbf{y} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{x}\mathbf{W}^{\top} + \mathbf{b}\right)
$$

where $\sigma$ is one of the supported pointwise activations.

**Inference mode** dispatches to a single BLAS + Accelerate pass that
avoids allocating the intermediate pre-activation tensor.  **Training
mode** falls back to unfused, differentiable ops so that the autograd
engine can compute correct gradients through both the linear projection
and the activation.

parameters.name: in_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of each input sample ($d_{\text{in}}$).

parameters.name: out_features
parameters.description: Dimensionality of each output sample ($d_{\text{out}}$).

parameters.name: activation
parameters.description:
Fused activation function.  Supported values:

* `'relu'` (default) — rectified linear unit,
  $\sigma(z) = \max(0, z)$.  Fastest; preferred for
  intermediate hidden layers.
* `'gelu'` — Gaussian error linear unit (tanh approximation),
  $\sigma(z) = z \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \left[1 + \tanh\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{\pi}} \left(z + 0.044715 z^3\right)\right)\right]$.
  Preferred in Transformer MLP blocks.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True` (default) a learnable bias is added before the
activation.  When `bias=False` the fused kernel is unavailable
and the layer falls back to standard unfused ops even during
inference.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for initial parameters.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Dtype for initial parameters.

examples:
Inference with ReLU activation:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.FusedLinear(64, 256, activation='relu')
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 64)
>>> with lucid.no_grad():
...     y = m(x)   # single-pass fused kernel on CPU
>>> y.shape
(4, 256)
GELU activation for a Transformer MLP block:
>>> mlp = nn.FusedLinear(768, 3072, activation='gelu')
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 16, 768)   # (batch, seq_len, d_model)
>>> with lucid.no_grad():
...     out = mlp(x)
>>> out.shape
(2, 16, 3072)
notes:
- **Input**: $(\ast, d_{\text{in}})$.
- **Output**: $(\ast, d_{\text{out}})$ after activation.

The kernel fusion benefit is most pronounced for **inference-only
workloads** (e.g. model serving with `lucid.no_grad()`).  During
training, the unfused fallback ensures that every intermediate value
needed for backpropagation is materialised correctly.

For `bias=False` the fused path is always skipped; prefer using
`bias=True` to take advantage of fusion.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Weight matrix of shape `(out_features, in_features)`.
Initialized with Kaiming uniform (same scheme as `Linear`).

attributes.name: activation
attributes.description: Name of the fused activation (`'relu'` or `'gelu'`).

methods.summary: Initialise the Conv1d module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the convolution to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{in}}, *)$.

returns.description:
Output tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{out}}, *)$ with
spatial dimensions determined by stride, padding, dilation, and
kernel size.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Applies a 1D convolution over a sequence of input signals.
members.extended:
A 1D convolution slides a learned filter (kernel) across a 1D sequence
and computes a dot product at each position.  Strictly speaking the
operation is *cross-correlation*:

$$
y[n, c_{\text{out}}, l] = \sum_{c_{\text{in}}=0}^{C_{\text{in}}/g - 1}
    \sum_{k=0}^{K-1}
    x\!\left[n,\, c_{\text{in}},\, l \cdot s + k \cdot d\right]
    \cdot W\!\left[c_{\text{out}},\, c_{\text{in}},\, k\right]
    + b\!\left[c_{\text{out}}\right]
$$

where $s$ is the stride, $d$ is the dilation factor,
$g$ is the number of groups, and $K$ is the kernel size.

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels in the input signal.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description:
Number of channels produced by the convolution (i.e. number of
independent filters).

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Length of the 1D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description:
Step size between consecutive kernel placements along the length
dimension. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
Zero-padding added to both sides of the input before the
convolution.  Can also be `"same"` (output length equals
`ceil(L_in / stride)`, requires `stride=1`) or `"valid"`
(no padding; identical to `padding=0`). Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description:
Spacing between kernel elements.  A dilation of `d` inserts
`d - 1` zeros between consecutive kernel weights, expanding the
effective receptive field without increasing parameter count
(also called *atrous* convolution). Default: `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description:
Splits the input and output channels into `groups` independent
paths.  `in_channels` and `out_channels` must both be
divisible by `groups`.  Setting `groups = in_channels`
gives *depthwise* convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias of shape `(out_channels,)` is
added to the output. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: padding_mode
parameters.description:
Padding strategy when `padding` is an integer.  One of
`"zeros"`, `"reflect"`, `"replicate"`, or `"circular"`.
Default: `"zeros"`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate parameters. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for the parameters. Default: `None`.

examples:
Basic 1D convolution:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> conv = nn.Conv1d(in_channels=1, out_channels=8, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(4, 1, 16)   # (N, C_in, L)
>>> y = conv(x)
>>> y.shape
(4, 8, 16)
Depthwise 1D convolution (one filter per channel):
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> depthwise = nn.Conv1d(
...     in_channels=32, out_channels=32,
...     kernel_size=5, padding=2, groups=32
... )
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 32, 64)
>>> y = depthwise(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 64)
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, L)$
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, L_{\text{out}})$ where



$$
L_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor
    \frac{L + 2p - d(K - 1) - 1}{s} + 1
\right\rfloor
$$

    and $p$ is the (symmetric) padding, $d$ is dilation,
    $K$ is kernel size, $s$ is stride.

**Groups and depthwise convolution.**  When `groups = g > 1`, the
input channels are split into `g` groups of size
`in_channels // g`, each group is convolved independently, and the
results are concatenated.  Depthwise convolution (`groups =
in_channels`) applies one filter per input channel, dramatically
reducing the parameter count compared to a full convolution.

**Dilated (atrous) convolution.**  Setting `dilation > 1` inserts
gaps between kernel taps, enlarging the receptive field without
additional parameters or pooling.  This is commonly used in semantic
segmentation and WaveNet-style audio models.

**Padding modes.**  `"reflect"` and `"circular"` padding
avoid boundary artefacts that zero-padding can introduce;
`"replicate"` repeats edge values.  These modes are applied via
a pre-padding step followed by a `padding=0` convolution.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable filter tensor of shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, kernel_size)`.
Initialized with Kaiming uniform:



$$
\text{fan\_in} = \frac{C_{\text{in}}}{g} \cdot K, \quad
W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[
    -\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{\text{fan\_in}}},\;
    \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{\text{fan\_in}}}
\right]
$$

(using $a = \sqrt{5}$ in the Kaiming formula).

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
Learnable bias of shape `(out_channels,)`, or `None` if
`bias=False`.

methods.summary: Initialise the Conv2d module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the convolution to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{in}}, *)$.

returns.description:
Output tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{out}}, *)$ with
spatial dimensions determined by stride, padding, dilation, and
kernel size.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Applies a 2D convolution over a batch of images or feature maps.
members.extended:
This module computes the 2D *cross-correlation* between the input
and a set of learnable filters.  For a single output channel and a
single input channel the operation is:

$$
y[n, c_{\text{out}}, h, w] =
\sum_{c_{\text{in}}=0}^{C_{\text{in}}/g - 1}
\sum_{k_h=0}^{K_H-1} \sum_{k_w=0}^{K_W-1}
x\!\left[n,\; c_{\text{in}},\;
          h \cdot s_h + k_h \cdot d_h,\;
          w \cdot s_w + k_w \cdot d_w\right]
\cdot W\!\left[c_{\text{out}},\; c_{\text{in}},\; k_h,\; k_w\right]
+ b\!\left[c_{\text{out}}\right]
$$

where $(s_h, s_w)$ is the stride, $(d_h, d_w)$ is the
dilation factor, and $g$ is the number of groups.

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels in the input image.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels produced by the convolution.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description:
Size of the convolving kernel.  A single `int` is broadcast
to `(kernel_size, kernel_size)`.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride of the convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
Padding added to all four sides of the input.  `"same"` pads
so the output spatial size equals `ceil(H_in / s)` (requires
`stride=1`); `"valid"` means no padding. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description:
Spacing between kernel elements (atrous / dilated convolution).
Default: `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description:
Number of blocked connections from input channels to output
channels.  Both `in_channels` and `out_channels` must be
divisible by `groups`.  `groups = in_channels` gives
*depthwise* convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True`, adds a learnable bias to the output. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: padding_mode
parameters.description:
`"zeros"`, `"reflect"`, `"replicate"`, or `"circular"`.
Default: `"zeros"`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate parameters. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for the parameters. Default: `None`.

examples:
Basic image convolution:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> conv = nn.Conv2d(in_channels=3, out_channels=64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(8, 3, 32, 32)   # (N, C_in, H, W)
>>> y = conv(x)
>>> y.shape
(8, 64, 32, 32)
Depthwise separable convolution block:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> depthwise  = nn.Conv2d(32, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1, groups=32)
>>> pointwise  = nn.Conv2d(32, 64, kernel_size=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(4, 32, 16, 16)
>>> y = pointwise(depthwise(x))
>>> y.shape
(4, 64, 16, 16)
Dilated convolution (receptive field 9×9 with only 3×3 parameters):
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> dilated = nn.Conv2d(1, 1, kernel_size=3, padding=4, dilation=4)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(1, 1, 16, 16)
>>> y = dilated(x)
>>> y.shape
(1, 1, 16, 16)
Convolution without bias:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> conv_no_bias = nn.Conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size=1, bias=False)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 16, 8, 8)
>>> y = conv_no_bias(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 8, 8)
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, H, W)$
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$ where



$$
H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor
    \frac{H + 2p_h - d_h(K_H - 1) - 1}{s_h} + 1
\right\rfloor, \quad
W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor
    \frac{W + 2p_w - d_w(K_W - 1) - 1}{s_w} + 1
\right\rfloor
$$

**Groups and depthwise convolution.**  When `groups = in_channels`
each input channel is convolved with its own filter, yielding
*depthwise* convolution.  This is the building block of
MobileNet-style architectures.  A subsequent `groups=1` Conv2d
with kernel size 1 (pointwise convolution) forms a
*depthwise-separable* block.

**Dilated (atrous) convolution.**  `dilation > 1` enlarges the
receptive field of each kernel tap without increasing the number of
parameters or reducing the spatial resolution.  Widely used in
semantic segmentation (DeepLab) and generative models.

**padding="same".**  Mimics the `SAME` padding convention: output
is spatially identical in size to the input.  When the required
total padding is odd, the extra pixel is added on the bottom/right
side (low side gets `pad_total // 2`).  Requires `stride=1`.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable filter tensor of shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, K_H, K_W)`.
Initialized with Kaiming uniform using $a = \sqrt{5}$:



$$
\text{fan\_in} = \frac{C_{\text{in}}}{g} \cdot K_H \cdot K_W, \quad
W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[
    -\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{\text{fan\_in}}},\;
    \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{\text{fan\_in}}}
\right]
$$

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the convolution to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{in}}, *)$.

returns.description:
Output tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{out}}, *)$ with
spatial dimensions determined by stride, padding, dilation, and
kernel size.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Applies a 3D convolution over volumetric data (e.g. video or medical scans).

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels in the input volume.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels produced by the convolution.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description:
Size of the 3D convolving kernel `(K_D, K_H, K_W)`.
A single `int` is broadcast to all three dimensions.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride along each spatial dimension. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.annotation: int, tuple[int, int, int], or str
parameters.description:
Zero-padding added on both sides along each spatial dimension.
Accepts `"same"` (requires `stride=1`) or `"valid"`.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description:
Number of blocked connections.  `groups = in_channels` gives
depthwise 3D convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True`, adds a learnable bias. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: padding_mode
parameters.description:
`"zeros"`, `"reflect"`, `"replicate"`, or `"circular"`.
Default: `"zeros"`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate parameters. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for the parameters. Default: `None`.

examples:
Basic volumetric convolution:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> conv3 = nn.Conv3d(in_channels=1, out_channels=16,
...                   kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 1, 16, 32, 32)   # (N, C, D, H, W)
>>> y = conv3(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 16, 16, 32, 32)
Strided 3D convolution for spatial downsampling:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> conv3_stride = nn.Conv3d(16, 32, kernel_size=3, stride=2, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 16, 16, 32, 32)
>>> y = conv3_stride(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 8, 16, 16)
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, D, H, W)$
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, D_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$ where



$$
X_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor
    \frac{X + 2p_x - d_x(K_X - 1) - 1}{s_x} + 1
\right\rfloor \quad \text{for } X \in \{D, H, W\}
$$

**Typical use cases.**  Conv3d is the standard building block for
video understanding (3D ResNets, SlowFast), medical image analysis
(CT/MRI volumetric segmentation), and point-cloud processing.  It is
computationally heavier than Conv2d by a factor of roughly
$K_D$ per layer; factorised (2+1)D convolutions are a common
approximation.

**Memory.**  A single 3D feature map can be large; consider
`groups > 1` or smaller `kernel_size` when memory is a concern.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable filter tensor of shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, K_D, K_H, K_W)`.
Initialized with Kaiming uniform:



$$
\text{fan\_in} = \frac{C_{\text{in}}}{g}
                 \cdot K_D \cdot K_H \cdot K_W, \quad
W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[
    -\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{\text{fan\_in}}},\;
    \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{\text{fan\_in}}}
\right]
$$

parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{in}}, *)$.

members.summary: Applies a 1D transposed convolution (fractionally-strided convolution).
members.extended:
A transposed convolution — sometimes loosely called a *deconvolution*,
though it is not a true mathematical inverse — is the gradient of a
standard convolution with respect to its input.  It upsamples the
spatial dimension by inserting implicit zeros between input elements
before applying a convolution, and is the go-to building block for
decoders, generators, and any network that must increase sequence
length.

Formally, for a stride $s$ and kernel size $K$:

$$
L_{\text{out}} = (L_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s
                 - 2p + d(K - 1) + p_{\text{out}} + 1
$$

where $p$ is `padding`, $d$ is `dilation`, and
$p_{\text{out}}$ is `output_padding`.

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels in the input.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels produced by the transposed convolution.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description:
Stride of the convolution.  Values `> 1` upsample the input.
Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
`dilation * (kernel_size - 1) - padding` zero-padding is added
to both sides of each dimension in the input.  Default: `0`.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Additional size added to one side of the output shape.  Must
satisfy `0 <= output_padding < max(stride, dilation)`.  Used
to disambiguate the output size when the formula
$(L_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s - 2p + d(K-1) + 1$ is
compatible with multiple $L_{\text{in}}$. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True`, adds a learnable bias. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate parameters. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for the parameters. Default: `None`.

examples:
Upsample a sequence by factor 2:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> upsample = nn.ConvTranspose1d(in_channels=16, out_channels=16,
...                               kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 16, 32)
>>> y = upsample(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 16, 64)
Simple 1D decoder layer:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> decoder = nn.ConvTranspose1d(64, 32, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(4, 64, 20)
>>> y = decoder(x)
>>> y.shape
(4, 32, 20)
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, L)$
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, L_{\text{out}})$ where



$$
L_{\text{out}} = (L - 1) \cdot s - 2p + d(K - 1)
                 + p_{\text{out}} + 1
$$

**Not a true inverse.**  `ConvTranspose1d` is the transpose
(adjoint) of `Conv1d` in the linear-algebra sense: if
`conv_forward` maps $\mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{in}} \times L} \to \mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{out}} \times L'}$, then
`ConvTranspose1d` with the same weights maps back
$\mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{out}} \times L'} \to \mathbb{R}^{C_{\text{in}} \times L}$.  When used in autoencoders or
flow models the weights are learned independently and no exact
inversion is implied.

**output_padding.**  The transposed convolution output size formula
can map multiple `L_in` values to the same `L_out`.
`output_padding` breaks this ambiguity by adding a single extra row
on the output; it does *not* add actual padding to the input.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable kernel of shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, kernel_size)`.
Note the channel axis ordering is **transposed** relative to
`Conv1d`: the leading dimension corresponds to
`in_channels`, not `out_channels`.
Initialized with Kaiming uniform ($a = \sqrt{5}$).

members.summary: Applies a 2D transposed convolution (fractionally-strided convolution).
members.extended:
Also known as a *fractionally-strided convolution*, this module is
commonly used as the spatial upsampling primitive in generative
models (VAEs, GANs), dense prediction decoders (U-Net), and
super-resolution networks.  It is the transpose (adjoint) of
`Conv2d`.

The output spatial dimensions satisfy:

$$
H_{\text{out}} = (H_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s_h - 2p_h
                 + d_h(K_H - 1) + p^{\text{out}}_h + 1

W_{\text{out}} = (W_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s_w - 2p_w
                 + d_w(K_W - 1) + p^{\text{out}}_w + 1
$$

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels in the input feature map.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels produced by the transposed convolution.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description:
Stride.  Values `> 1` upsample the spatial dimensions.
Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
`dilation * (kernel_size - 1) - padding` zero-padding is added
to both sides of each spatial dimension.  Default: `0`.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Additional size added to one side of each spatial dimension of
the output.  Must satisfy
`0 <= output_padding < max(stride, dilation)` along each axis.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True`, adds a learnable bias. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate parameters. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for the parameters. Default: `None`.

examples:
VAE decoder: upsample 4×4 latent to 8×8:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> decoder = nn.ConvTranspose2d(
...     in_channels=128, out_channels=64,
...     kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=1
... )
>>> z = lucid.zeros(8, 128, 4, 4)
>>> y = decoder(z)
>>> y.shape
(8, 64, 8, 8)
U-Net upsampling block:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> up = nn.ConvTranspose2d(256, 128, kernel_size=2, stride=2)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(4, 256, 16, 16)
>>> y = up(x)
>>> y.shape
(4, 128, 32, 32)
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, H, W)$
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$
    as given by the formulas above.

**Checkerboard artefacts.**  Transposed convolutions with
`stride > 1` can produce characteristic checkerboard patterns in
the output when kernel size is not divisible by stride.  A common
mitigation is to use `kernel_size = stride * n` for some integer
`n`, or to replace the transposed conv with bilinear upsampling
followed by a regular convolution.

**output_padding.**  When `stride > 1` the output size formula is
not injective: multiple input sizes map to the same output size.
`output_padding` resolves this ambiguity and must be set
consistently with the encoder stride to reconstruct the exact spatial
dimensions.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable kernel of shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, K_H, K_W)`.
The leading axis is `in_channels` — the reverse of
`Conv2d`.
Initialized with Kaiming uniform ($a = \sqrt{5}$).

members.summary: Applies a 3D transposed convolution (fractionally-strided convolution).
members.extended:
The 3D extension of `ConvTranspose2d`.  It upsamples all
three spatial dimensions simultaneously and is used in volumetric
decoders such as 3D autoencoders, video generators, and medical
image synthesis.

Output size along each spatial axis $X \in \{D, H, W\}$:

$$
X_{\text{out}} = (X_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot s_x - 2p_x
                 + d_x(K_X - 1) + p^{\text{out}}_x + 1
$$

parameters.name: in_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels in the input volume.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of channels produced by the transposed convolution.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the 3D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description:
Stride.  Values `> 1` upsample the spatial dimensions.
Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
`dilation * (kernel_size - 1) - padding` zero-padding applied
on both sides of each axis.  Default: `0`.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Additional size added to one side of each spatial dimension.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: If `True`, adds a learnable bias. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate parameters. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for the parameters. Default: `None`.

examples:
Volumetric upsampling (2× along all spatial axes):
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> up3d = nn.ConvTranspose3d(
...     in_channels=64, out_channels=32,
...     kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=1
... )
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 64, 4, 8, 8)
>>> y = up3d(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 8, 16, 16)
3D autoencoder decoder block:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> decoder3d = nn.ConvTranspose3d(128, 64, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(1, 128, 8, 8, 8)
>>> y = decoder3d(x)
>>> y.shape
(1, 64, 8, 8, 8)
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, D, H, W)$
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, D_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$
    as given by the formula above.

**Memory.**  3D transposed convolutions produce large feature maps
at decoder stages.  Gradient checkpointing or smaller
`out_channels` values are often necessary when operating on
high-resolution volumes.

**Symmetric decoder design.**  Pair each `Conv3d` in the
encoder with a `ConvTranspose3d` having identical
`kernel_size`, `stride`, and `padding` in the decoder to
guarantee exact shape reconstruction.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable kernel of shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, K_D, K_H, K_W)`.
Leading axis is `in_channels` (same convention as
`ConvTranspose2d`).
Initialized with Kaiming uniform ($a = \sqrt{5}$).

parameters.description: Input tensor of shape $(N, C_{\text{in}}, *)$.

members.summary: A `Conv1d` that infers `in_channels` from the first input.
members.extended:
Lazy modules defer the allocation and initialization of `weight`
and `bias` until the first call to `forward` (or until a
compatible `state_dict` is loaded).  This removes the need to know
`in_channels` at construction time, simplifying sequential model
building and automatic architecture search.

Materialization happens exactly once: on the first `forward`
call the channel count is read from `x.shape[1]`, weights are
allocated and Kaiming-uniform initialized, and subsequent calls
behave identically to a fully-initialized `Conv1d`.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of output channels.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Length of the 1D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride of the convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
Zero-padding or `"same"` / `"valid"` string specifier.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias is added after materialization.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: padding_mode
parameters.description:
`"zeros"`, `"reflect"`, `"replicate"`, or `"circular"`.
Default: `"zeros"`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description:
Device used when allocating weights at materialization time.
Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

examples:
Lazy conv in a sequential model:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> model = nn.Sequential(
...     nn.LazyConv1d(out_channels=32, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
...     nn.ReLU(),
... )
>>> x = lucid.zeros(4, 16, 64)   # in_channels=16 inferred here
>>> y = model(x)
>>> y.shape
(4, 32, 64)
Check that in_channels was inferred:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy = nn.LazyConv1d(out_channels=8, kernel_size=5, padding=2)
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
None
>>> _ = lazy(lucid.zeros(1, 3, 20))
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
3
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, L)$ — $C_{\text{in}}$ is
    inferred automatically.
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, L_{\text{out}})$ — same formula as
    `Conv1d`.

**State-dict loading.**  Loading a `state_dict` that contains a
`weight` key of shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, kernel_size)` also triggers
materialization, so a lazy module can be restored from a checkpoint
without ever calling `forward`.

**groups constraint.**  `in_channels` (inferred at runtime) must
be divisible by `groups`; this is checked inside `_initialize`.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
`None` until first `forward`; afterwards shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, kernel_size)`.

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
`None` until first `forward`; afterwards shape
`(out_channels,)` if `bias=True`, else remains `None`.

attributes.name: in_channels
attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; set to the inferred value on
the first forward pass.

members.summary: A `Conv2d` that infers `in_channels` from the first input.
members.extended:
Lazy initialization allows building 2D convolutional networks without
knowing the input channel count in advance.  The module registers
`weight` and `bias` as `None` parameters and allocates them on
the first `forward` call, reading `C_in` from
`x.shape[1]`.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of output channels.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the 2D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride of the convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
Zero-padding or `"same"` / `"valid"` string specifier.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias is added after materialization.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: padding_mode
parameters.description:
`"zeros"`, `"reflect"`, `"replicate"`, or `"circular"`.
Default: `"zeros"`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

examples:
Dynamic channel inference in a feature extractor:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> model = nn.Sequential(
...     nn.LazyConv2d(out_channels=64, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
...     nn.ReLU(),
...     nn.LazyConv2d(out_channels=128, kernel_size=3, padding=1),
... )
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 3, 32, 32)   # in_channels=3 inferred here
>>> y = model(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 128, 32, 32)
Verify inferred in_channels after forward:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy = nn.LazyConv2d(out_channels=16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> _ = lazy(lucid.zeros(1, 8, 16, 16))
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
8
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, H, W)$ — $C_{\text{in}}$ is
    inferred automatically.
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$ —
    same formula as `Conv2d`.

**State-dict materialization.**  Loading a `state_dict` with a
4-D `weight` tensor triggers materialization before parameter
copying, enabling round-trip checkpoint compatibility.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, K_H, K_W)` afterwards.

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; shape `(out_channels,)` if
`bias=True`, else remains `None`.

attributes.name: in_channels
attributes.description:
`None` before the first forward pass; inferred channel count
afterwards.

members.summary: A `Conv3d` that infers `in_channels` from the first input.
members.extended:
Lazy initialization for 3D convolution: `in_channels` need not be
specified at construction time.  Weight allocation and Kaiming
uniform initialization are deferred until the first `forward`
call (or `state_dict` load), at which point `C_in` is read from
`x.shape[1]`.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of output channels.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the 3D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride of the convolution. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.annotation: int, tuple[int, int, int], or str
parameters.description:
Zero-padding or `"same"` / `"valid"` string specifier.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias is added after materialization.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: padding_mode
parameters.description:
`"zeros"`, `"reflect"`, `"replicate"`, or `"circular"`.
Default: `"zeros"`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

examples:
Lazy 3D conv in a volumetric network:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy3d = nn.LazyConv3d(out_channels=32, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 4, 16, 32, 32)   # in_channels=4 inferred
>>> y = lazy3d(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 16, 32, 32)
Verify materialization:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy3d = nn.LazyConv3d(out_channels=16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> print(lazy3d.in_channels)
None
>>> _ = lazy3d(lucid.zeros(1, 8, 4, 4, 4))
>>> print(lazy3d.in_channels)
8
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, D, H, W)$ — $C_{\text{in}}$ is
    inferred automatically.
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, D_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$ —
    same formula as `Conv3d`.

**State-dict materialization.**  A 5-D `weight` tensor in the
`state_dict` triggers materialization before parameter copying.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, K_D, K_H, K_W)` afterwards.

members.summary: A `ConvTranspose1d` that infers `in_channels` from the first input.
members.extended:
Combines the fractionally-strided upsampling of
`ConvTranspose1d` with lazy weight materialization.
`in_channels` need not be known at construction time; it is read
from `x.shape[1]` on the first `forward` call.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of output channels after the transposed convolution.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Length of the 1D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride (upsampling factor). Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
`dilation * (kernel_size - 1) - padding` zero-padding on each
side.  String padding is not supported. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description: Additional size added to one side of the output. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias is added after materialization.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

examples:
Lazy transposed conv in a sequence decoder:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> decoder = nn.LazyConvTranspose1d(
...     out_channels=16, kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=1
... )
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 32, 10)   # in_channels=32 inferred
>>> y = decoder(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 16, 20)
Inspect pre- and post-materialization state:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy = nn.LazyConvTranspose1d(out_channels=8, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
None
>>> _ = lazy(lucid.zeros(1, 4, 16))
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
4
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, L)$ — $C_{\text{in}}$ is
    inferred automatically.
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, L_{\text{out}})$ — same formula as
    `ConvTranspose1d`.

**State-dict loading.**  A 3-D `weight` tensor in the
`state_dict` whose leading axis equals the inferred `in_channels`
triggers materialization before parameter copying.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, kernel_size)` afterwards.
Note: leading axis is `in_channels`, not `out_channels`.

members.extended:
Lazy version of the 2D transposed convolution.  Weight allocation is
deferred until the first `forward` call, making it easy to
compose upsampling decoder networks without manually tracking the
channel dimension through each block.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of output channels.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the 2D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride (upsampling factor). Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
`dilation * (kernel_size - 1) - padding` zero-padding applied
on each side.  String padding is not supported. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Additional size added to one side of each output dimension.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias is added after materialization.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

examples:
Lazy VAE decoder:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> decoder = nn.LazyConvTranspose2d(
...     out_channels=64, kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=1
... )
>>> z = lucid.zeros(4, 128, 8, 8)   # in_channels=128 inferred
>>> y = decoder(z)
>>> y.shape
(4, 64, 16, 16)
Verify inference:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy = nn.LazyConvTranspose2d(out_channels=32, kernel_size=3)
>>> _ = lazy(lucid.zeros(1, 64, 4, 4))
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
64
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, H, W)$ — $C_{\text{in}}$ is
    inferred automatically.
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$ —
    same formula as `ConvTranspose2d`.

**State-dict loading.**  A 4-D `weight` tensor (shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, K_H, K_W)`) triggers
materialization before parameter copying.

**Symmetric encoder–decoder pairs.**  Pair each `LazyConv2d`
encoder stride with a `LazyConvTranspose2d` of matching
`kernel_size`, `stride`, and `padding` to reconstruct exact
spatial dimensions.

attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, K_H, K_W)` afterwards.

members.summary: A `ConvTranspose3d` that infers `in_channels` from the first input.
members.extended:
Lazy version of the 3D transposed convolution.  Combines volumetric
upsampling with deferred weight allocation, simplifying the
construction of 3D generative decoders and medical image synthesis
networks where channel dimensions are computed dynamically.

parameters.name: out_channels
parameters.description: Number of output channels.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the 3D convolving kernel.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description: Stride (upsampling factor). Default: `1`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
`dilation * (kernel_size - 1) - padding` zero-padding applied
on each side of each spatial axis.  String padding is not
supported. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: output_padding
parameters.description:
Additional size added to one side of each spatial output
dimension. Default: `0`.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description: Number of blocked connections. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
If `True`, a learnable bias is added after materialization.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description: Spacing between kernel elements. Default: `1`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type used when allocating weights. Default: `None`.

examples:
Lazy 3D decoder block:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> up3d = nn.LazyConvTranspose3d(
...     out_channels=32, kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=1
... )
>>> x = lucid.zeros(2, 64, 4, 8, 8)   # in_channels=64 inferred
>>> y = up3d(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 32, 8, 16, 16)
Verify materialization:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lazy = nn.LazyConvTranspose3d(out_channels=16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
None
>>> _ = lazy(lucid.zeros(1, 8, 4, 4, 4))
>>> print(lazy.in_channels)
8
notes:
Input:
    $(N, C_{\text{in}}, D, H, W)$ — $C_{\text{in}}$ is
    inferred automatically.
Output:
    $(N, C_{\text{out}}, D_{\text{out}}, H_{\text{out}}, W_{\text{out}})$ —
    same formula as `ConvTranspose3d`.

**State-dict loading.**  A 5-D `weight` tensor triggers
materialization before parameter copying.

**Symmetric 3D encoder–decoder.**  Pair each `LazyConv3d`
with a `LazyConvTranspose3d` using matching `kernel_size`,
`stride`, and `padding` for lossless spatial reconstruction.

attributes.description:
`None` before materialization; shape
`(in_channels, out_channels // groups, K_D, K_H, K_W)`
afterwards.
