members.summary: Rectified Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0,\, x)
$$

The simplest non-linear activation — sets all negative values to zero.
Commonly used in hidden layers of deep networks because it avoids the
vanishing-gradient problem that affects sigmoid and tanh for large inputs,
and is inexpensive to compute.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description:
If `True`, the operation modifies the input tensor in-place,
saving memory allocation.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.ReLU()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([0., 0., 1., 2.])
>>> # Applied to a batch of feature vectors; negatives zeroed, shape preserved
>>> m = nn.ReLU(inplace=True)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 64)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.name: LeakyReLU
members.signature: LeakyReLU(negative_slope: float = 0.01, inplace: bool = False)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the LeakyReLU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

members.summary: Leaky Rectified Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{LeakyReLU}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\
    \alpha \cdot x & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Unlike standard ReLU, neurons with negative pre-activations receive a
small gradient $\alpha$ during back-propagation, avoiding the
"dying ReLU" phenomenon where units become permanently inactive.

parameters.name: negative_slope
parameters.description: Slope $\alpha$ applied to negative inputs.  Default: `0.01`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: If `True`, modifies the input tensor in-place.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.LeakyReLU(negative_slope=0.1)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.2, -0.1,  0. ,  1. ,  2. ])
>>> # Default slope 0.01 — very small leak
>>> m = nn.LeakyReLU()
>>> x = lucid.randn(3, 32)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(3, 32)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.summary: Exponential Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{ELU}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x & \text{if } x > 0 \\
    \alpha \left(e^{x} - 1\right) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

ELU smoothly saturates to $-\alpha$ for large negative inputs,
producing mean activations closer to zero than ReLU.  This self-normalising
tendency can accelerate convergence in deep networks.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Scale $\alpha$ for the negative exponential branch.
Default: `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: If `True`, modifies the input tensor in-place.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.ELU(alpha=1.0)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.8647, -0.6321,  0.    ,  1.    ,  2.    ])
>>> # Custom alpha shifts the negative saturation floor
>>> m = nn.ELU(alpha=0.5)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 16)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(2, 16)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.name: CELU
members.signature: CELU(alpha: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = False)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the CELU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Continuously Differentiable Exponential Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{CELU}(x) =
\max(0,\, x)
+ \min\!\left(0,\; \alpha\!\left(e^{x/\alpha} - 1\right)\right)
$$

CELU differs from ELU in that it is continuously differentiable
everywhere, including at zero, by ensuring the left and right derivatives
match at $x = 0$.  Both branches coincide with an exponential
scaled by $\alpha$, and the output transitions smoothly from the
negative saturating region to the linear positive branch.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Scale $\alpha > 0$ controlling the slope of the negative branch
and the value to which it saturates.  Default: `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently unused.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.CELU(alpha=1.0)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.8647, -0.6321,  0.    ,  1.    ,  2.    ])
>>> # Smoother negative branch than ELU for gradient-sensitive architectures
>>> m = nn.CELU(alpha=0.5)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 128)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 128)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.name: SELU
members.signature: SELU(inplace: bool = False)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the SELU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: Scaled Exponential Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{SELU}(x) =
\lambda
\begin{cases}
    x & \text{if } x > 0 \\
    \alpha \left(e^{x} - 1\right) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $\lambda \approx 1.0507$ and $\alpha \approx 1.6733$
are fixed constants derived from the self-normalising property.  When
weights are initialised with `lecun_normal` and the network uses only
SELU activations, the mean and variance of each layer's output converge
to 0 and 1, enabling stable training of very deep fully-connected networks
without batch normalisation.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: If `True`, modifies the input tensor in-place.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.SELU()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-1.5202, -1.1113,  0.    ,  1.0507,  2.1014])
>>> # Suitable for deep fully-connected architectures
>>> layers = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(128, 64), nn.SELU(), nn.Linear(64, 10))
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 128)
>>> out = layers(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 10)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

The self-normalising guarantee holds strictly only for networks with
no skip connections, no convolutional layers, and Lecun-normal weight
initialisation.  In practice SELU is used more broadly as a drop-in
for ELU.

members.name: GELU
members.signature: GELU(approximate: GeluApproximate = 'none')

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the GELU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Gaussian Error Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{GELU}(x) = x \cdot \Phi(x)
$$

where $\Phi(x)$ is the cumulative distribution function of the
standard normal distribution.  Intuitively, GELU weights each input by
the probability that a standard Gaussian random variable is smaller than
it — inputs far into the positive tail pass through nearly unchanged,
while those deep in the negative tail are suppressed.

When `approximate="tanh"` the following closed-form approximation is
used instead:

$$
\text{GELU}(x) \approx
x \cdot \frac{1}{2}
\left[
    1 + \tanh\!\left(
        \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{\pi}}
        \left(x + 0.044715\, x^3\right)
    \right)
\right]
$$

parameters.name: approximate
parameters.description:
Approximation method.  `"none"` uses the exact erf-based formula;
`"tanh"` uses the faster tanh approximation.  Default: `"none"`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.GELU()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.0454, -0.1587,  0.    ,  0.8413,  1.9545])
>>> # Fast tanh approximation — nearly identical for most inputs
>>> m_approx = nn.GELU(approximate="tanh")
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 512)
>>> out = m_approx(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 512)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

GELU is the default activation in transformer architectures (BERT, GPT)
because its smooth non-linearity and non-zero gradient for all inputs
improve training stability over ReLU for attention-based models.

members.summary: Sigmoid Linear Unit (Swish) activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{SiLU}(x) = x \cdot \sigma(x)
= \frac{x}{1 + e^{-x}}
$$

where $\sigma$ is the logistic sigmoid function.  The gate
$\sigma(x)$ smoothly interpolates between 0 (deep negative) and
the identity (large positive), producing a non-monotone shape with a
small dip below zero near $x \approx -1.3$.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently unused.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.SiLU()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.2384, -0.2689,  0.    ,  0.7311,  1.7616])
>>> # Common backbone activation — shape-preserving
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 256)
>>> out = nn.SiLU()(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 256)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

SiLU / Swish is used in EfficientNet, MobileNetV3, and many modern
vision backbones as a smooth, self-gated alternative to ReLU.  It is
differentiable everywhere, including at zero.

members.summary: Mish activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Mish}(x) = x \cdot \tanh\!\bigl(\text{Softplus}(x)\bigr)
= x \cdot \tanh\!\bigl(\ln(1 + e^x)\bigr)
$$

Mish is smooth, non-monotone, and unbounded above while being bounded
below (approaching zero for large negative inputs).  It preserves small
negative values — unlike ReLU — and empirically outperforms Swish/SiLU on
several object detection benchmarks (YOLOv4, YOLOv5).
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Mish()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.1876, -0.3034,  0.    ,  0.8651,  1.9440])
>>> # Drop-in for SiLU in detection backbones
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 128, 7, 7)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 128, 7, 7)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

Mish requires computing both a softplus and a tanh, making it slightly
more expensive than ReLU or SiLU.  The smooth gradient landscape can
aid optimisation in very deep networks.

members.summary: Softplus activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Softplus}(x) = \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\bigl(1 + e^{\beta x}\bigr)
$$

For numerical stability the output falls back to the identity when
$\beta x > \text{threshold}$, avoiding overflow in the exponential.
Softplus is a smooth, everywhere-differentiable approximation of ReLU,
and is useful as the positivity-enforcing transformation in probabilistic
models (e.g. predicting variances or scale parameters).

parameters.name: beta
parameters.description:
Sharpness parameter $\beta$ controlling how closely the curve
approximates a hard hinge.  Larger values approach ReLU.
Default: `1.0`.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description:
Above this value of $\beta x$ the function falls back to the
identity to prevent overflow.  Default: `20.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Softplus()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([0.1269, 0.3133, 0.6931, 1.3133, 2.1269])
>>> # Sharper approximation with beta=5
>>> m_sharp = nn.Softplus(beta=5.0)
>>> x = lucid.randn(3, 64)
>>> out = m_sharp(x)
>>> out.shape
(3, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

Setting `beta=1` recovers the standard log-sum-exp formulation.
For very large `beta` the function becomes numerically equivalent to
ReLU almost everywhere.

members.summary: Hard Swish activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Hardswish}(x) = x \cdot \frac{\text{ReLU6}(x + 3)}{6}
$$

which is equivalent to:

$$
\text{Hardswish}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    0           & \text{if } x \leq -3 \\
    x(x+3)/6    & \text{if } -3 < x < 3 \\
    x           & \text{if } x \geq 3
\end{cases}
$$

Hard Swish approximates the SiLU (Swish) activation using only integer
arithmetic-friendly piecewise-linear operations, making it particularly
efficient on hardware without native sigmoid support (e.g. mobile NPUs).
It was introduced in MobileNetV3.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Hardswish()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-4.0, -1.5, 0.0, 1.5, 4.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([0.    , -0.375,  0.    ,  1.125,  4.    ])
>>> # Efficient mobile backbone activation
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 96, 28, 28)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(1, 96, 28, 28)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.summary: Hard Sigmoid activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Hardsigmoid}(x) = \max\!\left(0,\, \min\!\left(1,\, \frac{x}{6} + \frac{1}{2}\right)\right)
$$

This is a piecewise-linear approximation of the logistic sigmoid that
saturates to 0 for $x \leq -3$ and to 1 for $x \geq 3$,
with a linear ramp in between.  It avoids the exponential required by the
exact sigmoid, making it suitable for resource-constrained deployments.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Hardsigmoid()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-4.0, -3.0, 0.0, 3.0, 4.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([0.  , 0.  , 0.5 , 1.  , 1.  ])
>>> # Lightweight gating in mobile attention heads
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 32)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(2, 32)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in $[0, 1]$.

members.summary: Sigmoid (logistic) activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}
$$

Maps all real inputs to the open interval $(0, 1)$.  Commonly used
in the output layer of binary classifiers and as a gating function in
recurrent architectures (LSTM, GRU).
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Sigmoid()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([0.1192, 0.2689, 0.5   , 0.7311, 0.8808])
>>> # Binary classification output layer
>>> x = lucid.randn(16, 1)
>>> probs = m(x)   # probabilities in (0, 1)
>>> probs.shape
(16, 1)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in $(0, 1)$.

Sigmoid saturates for large absolute inputs, causing vanishing gradients
in deep networks.  For hidden layers, ReLU or GELU are generally preferred.

members.summary: Hyperbolic tangent activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}
$$

Maps all real inputs to the open interval $(-1, 1)$.  Zero-centred,
unlike sigmoid, which makes it the preferred gate/output activation in
many recurrent architectures (LSTM, GRU).
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Tanh()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.9640, -0.7616,  0.    ,  0.7616,  0.9640])
>>> # Hidden state output in a simple recurrent cell
>>> h = lucid.randn(32, 128)
>>> h_next = m(h)
>>> h_next.shape
(32, 128)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in $(-1, 1)$.

Like sigmoid, tanh saturates for large inputs.  Its zero-centred output
reduces the bias shift problem in successive layers, but the vanishing
gradient issue still applies for very deep networks.

members.summary: Softmax activation function.
members.extended:
Applies to each slice along `dim`:

$$
\text{Softmax}(x_i) = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}
$$

Normalises the input to a proper probability distribution: all outputs
are non-negative and sum to 1 along the specified dimension.  Used as
the final layer of multi-class classifiers and in attention mechanisms.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
The dimension along which softmax is computed.  Must be specified
explicitly for most use cases; `None` is retained for compatibility
but raises a warning at runtime.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Softmax(dim=-1)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> m(x)
tensor([[0.0900, 0.2447, 0.6652]])
>>> # Attention weight normalisation over sequence length
>>> scores = lucid.randn(4, 8, 64)   # (batch, heads, seq_len)
>>> weights = nn.Softmax(dim=-1)(scores)
>>> weights.shape
(4, 8, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input; values along `dim` sum
  to 1.

For numerical stability the implementation subtracts the maximum value
along `dim` before exponentiation (log-sum-exp trick), preventing
overflow without changing the result.

members.summary: Softmax activation applied over the channel dimension of a 4-D tensor.
members.extended:
Given a spatial feature map of shape $(N, C, H, W)$, applies
softmax along the channel axis $C$:

$$
\text{Softmax2d}(x)_{n,c,h,w}
= \frac{e^{x_{n,c,h,w}}}{\sum_{c'} e^{x_{n,c',h,w}}}
$$

Equivalent to `Softmax(dim=-3)` — channels are the third-from-last
axis.  Used in dense prediction heads (semantic segmentation, saliency
maps) where each spatial location's channel scores form a categorical
distribution.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Softmax2d()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 5, 4, 4)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(1, 5, 4, 4)
>>> # Verify that channel probabilities sum to 1 at each pixel
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 10, 8, 8)
>>> out = nn.Softmax2d()(x)
>>> # out.sum(dim=1) should be all-ones spatially
>>> out.sum(dim=1).shape
(2, 8, 8)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, H, W)$ — must be exactly 4-D.
- Output: $(N, C, H, W)$ — values along the channel dimension sum
  to 1 at every spatial location.

members.summary: Log-Softmax activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{LogSoftmax}(x_i)
= \ln\!\left(\frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}\right)
= x_i - \ln\!\sum_j e^{x_j}
$$

Combines the softmax normalisation with a logarithm in a single,
numerically stable pass.  The output lives in $(-\infty, 0]$ and
is intended to be paired with `lucid.nn.NLLLoss` for
multi-class classification, or equivalently used directly with
`lucid.nn.functional.cross_entropy`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
The dimension along which log-softmax is computed.  Must be specified
explicitly for most use cases.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.LogSoftmax(dim=-1)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> m(x)
tensor([[-2.4076, -1.4076, -0.4076]])
>>> # Classifier output layer — pair with NLLLoss
>>> logits = lucid.randn(32, 10)
>>> log_probs = nn.LogSoftmax(dim=-1)(logits)
>>> log_probs.shape
(32, 10)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input; values along `dim` are
  non-positive and represent log-probabilities.

members.summary: Randomized Leaky ReLU activation function.
members.extended:
During **training**, the negative slope for each element is drawn
independently and uniformly from $[\text{lower}, \text{upper}]$:

$$
\text{RReLU}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x          & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\
    a \cdot x  & \text{otherwise},\quad
                  a \sim \mathcal{U}(\text{lower},\, \text{upper})
\end{cases}
$$

During **evaluation**, the slope is fixed at the deterministic midpoint
$a = (\text{lower} + \text{upper}) / 2$.

The stochastic negative slope acts as a form of noise-based
regularisation, similar in spirit to Dropout, and was found to improve
generalisation in image classification tasks.

parameters.name: lower
parameters.description: Lower bound of the uniform slope distribution.  Default: `1/8`.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: Upper bound of the uniform slope distribution.  Default: `1/3`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: If `True`, modifies the input tensor in-place.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.RReLU(lower=0.1, upper=0.3)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> out_train = m(x)          # slope sampled from U(0.1, 0.3)
>>> m.eval()
>>> out_eval = m(x)           # slope fixed at 0.2
>>> out_eval.shape
(5,)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

Call `m.train()` / `m.eval()` to switch between stochastic and
deterministic modes respectively.  The module inherits training-mode
tracking from `lucid.nn.Module`.

members.summary: Cosine similarity between two tensors along a specified dimension.
members.extended:
Computes element-wise cosine similarity:

$$
\text{CosineSimilarity}(x_1, x_2)
= \frac{x_1 \cdot x_2}{\max\!\bigl(\|x_1\|_2 \cdot \|x_2\|_2,\; \varepsilon\bigr)}
$$

The result is a scalar (or tensor with `dim` reduced) in the range
$[-1, 1]$, where $1$ indicates identical direction, $0$
orthogonality, and $-1$ opposite direction.  The `eps` floor
on the denominator prevents division by zero for zero-norm vectors.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension along which cosine similarity is computed.  Default: `1`.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small value $\varepsilon$ added to the denominator for
numerical stability.  Default: `1e-8`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.CosineSimilarity(dim=1)
>>> x1 = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])
>>> x2 = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0], [1.0, 0.0]])
>>> m(x1, x2)
tensor([1., 0.])
>>> # Comparing sentence embeddings along the feature dimension
>>> emb1 = lucid.randn(32, 768)
>>> emb2 = lucid.randn(32, 768)
>>> sim = nn.CosineSimilarity(dim=1)(emb1, emb2)
>>> sim.shape
(32,)
notes:
- Input `x1`: $(N, D)$ or any shape broadcastable to `x2`.
- Input `x2`: $(N, D)$ or any shape broadcastable to `x1`.
- Output: $(N,)$ — `dim` is reduced; same batch dimensions as
  input.

members.summary: Pairwise $\ell_p$ distance between corresponding rows of two tensors.
members.extended:
Computes the $\ell_p$ norm of the difference vector for each pair
of rows:

$$
d_p(x_1, x_2)
= \left\| x_1 - x_2 + \varepsilon \right\|_p
= \left( \sum_i \left| x_{1,i} - x_{2,i} + \varepsilon \right|^p \right)^{1/p}
$$

A small $\varepsilon$ is added before the norm computation for
numerical stability.  With `p=2` this yields the standard Euclidean
distance; with `p=1` the Manhattan distance.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: The exponent $p$ of the $\ell_p$ norm.  Default: `2.0`.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small $\varepsilon$ added to the difference for numerical
stability.  Default: `1e-6`.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description:
If `True`, the reduced dimension is retained as a size-1 dimension
in the output.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.PairwiseDistance(p=2.0)
>>> x1 = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])
>>> x2 = lucid.tensor([[0.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.0]])
>>> m(x1, x2)
tensor([1.0000, 1.0000])
>>> # Euclidean distance for embedding comparison in a Siamese network
>>> emb1 = lucid.randn(64, 128)
>>> emb2 = lucid.randn(64, 128)
>>> dist = nn.PairwiseDistance(p=2.0)(emb1, emb2)
>>> dist.shape
(64,)
notes:
- Input `x1`: $(N, D)$.
- Input `x2`: $(N, D)$.
- Output: $(N,)$ if `keepdim=False`, else $(N, 1)$.

Commonly used in Siamese networks and metric-learning objectives such as
contrastive loss and triplet loss, where the distance between paired
embeddings must be differentiable.

members.summary: ReLU6 activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{ReLU6}(x) = \min\!\bigl(\max(0,\, x),\, 6\bigr)
$$

A capped variant of ReLU that clamps activations to the range
$[0, 6]$.  The hard cap prevents activations from growing
unboundedly, which can improve robustness of fixed-point quantisation
(8-bit or lower) by keeping the dynamic range bounded.  Widely used in
mobile architectures such as MobileNetV1 and MobileNetV2.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently unused.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.ReLU6()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, 0.0, 3.0, 6.0, 10.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([0., 0., 3., 6., 6.])
>>> # Quantisation-friendly activation in depthwise-separable convolutions
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 32, 14, 14)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 32, 14, 14)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in $[0, 6]$.

members.summary: Parametric Rectified Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{PReLU}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x            & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\
    \alpha \cdot x & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Unlike LeakyReLU, the slope $\alpha$ is a **learnable parameter**
updated during back-propagation.  A single shared slope can be used for
all channels (`num_parameters=1`) or each channel can have its own
slope (`num_parameters=num_channels`).

parameters.name: num_parameters
parameters.description:
Number of learnable slopes.  Use `1` for a single shared slope or
set to the number of input channels for per-channel slopes.
Default: `1`.

parameters.name: init
parameters.description: Initial value for all slope parameters.  Default: `0.25`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description:
Device on which the parameter tensor is allocated.  Default: `None`
(uses the default device).

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description:
Data type of the parameter tensor.  Default: `None` (uses the
default floating-point type).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.PReLU(num_parameters=1, init=0.25)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.5, -0.25,  0.  ,  1.  ,  2.  ])
>>> # Per-channel slopes for a feature map with 64 channels
>>> m = nn.PReLU(num_parameters=64)
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 64, 16, 16)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 64, 16, 16)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

When `num_parameters > 1`, the input is expected to have the channel
dimension second (i.e. shape $(N, C, *)$), and `num_parameters`
must equal $C$.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable negative slopes $\alpha$.  Updated by the optimiser
during training.

members.name: Threshold
members.signature: Threshold(threshold: float, value: float, inplace: bool = False)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(threshold: float, value: float, inplace: bool = False)
methods.summary: Initialise the Threshold module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: Threshold activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Threshold}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x               & \text{if } x > \text{threshold} \\
    \text{value}    & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Values that fall at or below the threshold are replaced with a constant
`value`.  This generalises the standard ReLU (threshold=0, value=0) to
arbitrary cut-off points and fill values.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description:
The cut-off value.  Elements strictly greater than this pass through
unchanged.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The replacement constant for elements that do not exceed the threshold.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently unused.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Threshold(threshold=1.0, value=-1.0)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-1. , -1. ,  1.5,  2. ])
>>> # Use as a generalised ReLU with a non-zero floor
>>> m = nn.Threshold(threshold=0.0, value=0.0)
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 32)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 32)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.name: Hardtanh
members.signature: Hardtanh(min_val: float = -1.0, max_val: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = False)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(min_val: float = -1.0, max_val: float = 1.0, inplace: bool = False)
methods.summary: Initialise the Hardtanh module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Hardtanh activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Hardtanh}(x) = \text{clamp}(x,\; \text{min\_val},\; \text{max\_val})
=
\begin{cases}
    \text{min\_val} & \text{if } x < \text{min\_val} \\
    x               & \text{if } \text{min\_val} \leq x \leq \text{max\_val} \\
    \text{max\_val} & \text{if } x > \text{max\_val}
\end{cases}
$$

A piecewise-linear activation that clips the input to a fixed interval.
With the default `[-1, 1]` range it approximates the tanh function using
only comparisons and clamps, making it suitable for quantised models.

parameters.name: min_val
parameters.description: Lower bound of the clamping range.  Default: `-1.0`.

parameters.name: max_val
parameters.description: Upper bound of the clamping range.  Default: `1.0`.

parameters.name: inplace
parameters.description: Accepted for API compatibility; currently unused.  Default: `False`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Hardtanh(min_val=-1.0, max_val=1.0)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-3.0, -0.5, 0.0, 0.5, 3.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-1. , -0.5,  0. ,  0.5,  1. ])
>>> # Custom range for output normalisation
>>> m = nn.Hardtanh(min_val=0.0, max_val=6.0)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 64)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in
  $[\text{min\_val},\; \text{max\_val}]$.

`ReLU6` is a special case of Hardtanh with `min_val=0` and
`max_val=6`.

members.name: LogSigmoid
members.signature: LogSigmoid()

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: Log-Sigmoid activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{LogSigmoid}(x)
= \ln\!\left(\sigma(x)\right)
= \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}\right)
= -\ln\!\bigl(1 + e^{-x}\bigr)
$$

The output is always $\leq 0$.  Useful as a numerically stable
log-probability output for binary models, or when computing negative
log-likelihood loss without a separate sigmoid step.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.LogSigmoid()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-2.1269, -1.3133, -0.6931, -0.3133, -0.1269])
>>> # Log-probability output for binary classification
>>> x = lucid.randn(32, 1)
>>> log_probs = m(x)   # values in (-inf, 0]
>>> log_probs.shape
(32, 1)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in $(-\infty, 0]$.

The implementation fuses the log and sigmoid into a single engine call
to avoid materialising the intermediate sigmoid tensor and to ensure
numerical accuracy for large negative inputs.

members.name: Softsign
members.signature: Softsign()

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: Softsign activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Softsign}(x) = \frac{x}{1 + |x|}
$$

Maps all real inputs to the open interval $(-1, 1)$, similar to
tanh but with polynomial rather than exponential saturation — meaning the
function approaches its limits more slowly and gradients remain larger for
moderately large inputs.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Softsign()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-4.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 4.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-0.8   , -0.5   ,  0.    ,  0.5   ,  0.8   ])
>>> # Slower saturation compared to tanh
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 64)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input, values in $(-1, 1)$.

Softsign saturates more slowly than tanh, so it is less prone to
vanishing gradients for large activations.  It is sometimes used as a
tanh substitute in recurrent networks.

members.name: Softmin
members.signature: Softmin(dim: int | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the Softmin module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Softmin activation function.
members.extended:
Applies softmax to the negated input along a specified dimension:

$$
\text{Softmin}(x_i) = \frac{e^{-x_i}}{\sum_j e^{-x_j}}
$$

Equivalent to `Softmax(-x)`.  The result is a valid probability
distribution where **lower** values receive **higher** weight — the
inverse of softmax.  Useful when scores represent costs or distances
rather than affinities.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: The dimension along which softmin is computed.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Softmin(dim=-1)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])
>>> m(x)
tensor([[0.6652, 0.2447, 0.0900]])
>>> # Lowest-cost option gets the highest weight
>>> costs = lucid.tensor([[0.1, 0.5, 0.9]])
>>> weights = nn.Softmin(dim=-1)(costs)
>>> weights.shape
(1, 3)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input; values along `dim` are
  non-negative and sum to 1.

members.name: GLU
members.signature: GLU(dim: int = -1)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the GLU module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Gated Linear Unit activation function.
members.extended:
Splits the input tensor into two equal halves along `dim`, then
applies an element-wise gate:

$$
\text{GLU}(x) = x_1 \otimes \sigma(x_2)
$$

where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are the two halves of $x$ along
`dim`, $\otimes$ is element-wise multiplication, and
$\sigma$ is the logistic sigmoid.  The sigmoid gate controls how
much information from the first half flows through, enabling the network
to learn a soft feature-selection mechanism.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Dimension along which the input is split.  The size along this
dimension must be even.  Default: `-1`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.GLU(dim=-1)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0]])   # split into [1,2] and [3,4]
>>> m(x)
tensor([[0.9526, 1.9640]])
>>> # Feed-forward block with GLU gating
>>> ff = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(256, 512), nn.GLU(dim=-1))
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 64, 256)
>>> out = ff(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 64, 256)
notes:
- Input: $(\ldots,\; 2N,\; \ldots)$ — size along `dim` must be
  even.
- Output: $(\ldots,\; N,\; \ldots)$ — output is half the size of
  the input along `dim`.

GLU was introduced for language modelling with convolutional sequence
models and is also used in transformer feed-forward blocks as an
alternative to ReLU/GELU projection layers.

members.name: Hardshrink
members.signature: Hardshrink(lambd: float = 0.5)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the Hardshrink module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Hard Shrinkage activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Hardshrink}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x   & \text{if } |x| > \lambda \\
    0   & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Values within the band $[-\lambda, \lambda]$ are zeroed out
("shrunk" to zero), while large values pass through unchanged.
Hard shrinkage encourages sparse representations and is commonly used
in sparse coding and wavelet-based signal processing.

parameters.name: lambd
parameters.description: Threshold $\lambda \geq 0$.  Default: `0.5`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Hardshrink(lambd=0.5)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.0, -0.4, 0.0, 0.4, 1.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-1.,  0.,  0.,  0.,  1.])
>>> # Sparsifying activation for dictionary learning
>>> m = nn.Hardshrink(lambd=1.0)
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 64)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

members.name: Tanhshrink
members.signature: Tanhshrink()

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

members.summary: Tanhshrink activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Tanhshrink}(x) = x - \tanh(x)
$$

Subtracts the tanh of the input from the input itself.  Near zero the
output is approximately $x^3/3$ (the tanh Taylor residual), so the
function is smooth and cubic near the origin.  For large inputs,
$\tanh(x) \to \pm 1$, so the output approaches $x \mp 1$,
behaving asymptotically like the identity.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Tanhshrink()
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-2.0, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-1.0360, -0.2384,  0.    ,  0.2384,  1.0360])
>>> # Smooth sparse regularisation in an encoder
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 256)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 256)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

Tanhshrink is used as a soft thresholding operator in some sparse
representation and compressed-sensing networks.

members.name: Softshrink
members.signature: Softshrink(lambd: float = 0.5)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the Softshrink module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the activation function element-wise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor of arbitrary shape.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Soft Shrinkage activation function.
members.extended:
Applies element-wise:

$$
\text{Softshrink}(x) =
\begin{cases}
    x - \lambda & \text{if } x > \lambda \\
    x + \lambda & \text{if } x < -\lambda \\
    0           & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Shrinks all values towards zero by $\lambda$: values outside the
dead band are shifted, while values inside are zeroed.  This is the
proximal operator of the $\ell_1$ norm and appears in LASSO and
iterative soft-thresholding algorithms (ISTA / FISTA).

parameters.name: lambd
parameters.description: Threshold $\lambda \geq 0$.  Default: `0.5`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> m = nn.Softshrink(lambd=0.5)
>>> x = lucid.tensor([-1.5, -0.3, 0.0, 0.3, 1.5])
>>> m(x)
tensor([-1. ,  0. ,  0. ,  0. ,  1. ])
>>> # L1-proximal layer in an unrolled ISTA network
>>> m = nn.Softshrink(lambd=0.1)
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 128)
>>> out = m(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 128)
notes:
- Input: $(*)$ — any shape.
- Output: $(*)$ — same shape as input.

Unlike `Hardshrink`, Softshrink *shifts* surviving values toward
zero rather than preserving them exactly.  This makes it a continuous
function and gives it a well-defined subgradient everywhere.

members.name: LayerNorm
members.signature: LayerNorm(normalized_shape: int | list[int] | tuple[int, ...], eps: float = 1e-05, elementwise_affine: bool = True, bias: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(normalized_shape: int | list[int] | tuple[int, ...], eps: float = 1e-05, elementwise_affine: bool = True, bias: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
methods.summary: Initialise the LayerNorm module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply normalisation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor whose shape is documented in the class docstring.

returns.description: Normalised tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Layer normalization over the trailing dimensions of the input.
members.extended:
Normalises each sample independently by computing mean and variance
over the axes defined by `normalized_shape`:

$$
y = \frac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \varepsilon}} \cdot \gamma + \beta
$$

where $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are computed over the last
`len(normalized_shape)` dimensions of the input tensor.

Unlike batch normalization, Layer Norm does not depend on the batch
dimension, making it well-suited to variable-length sequences,
transformer architectures, and settings where the batch size may be
as small as 1.

parameters.name: normalized_shape
parameters.description:
Shape of the trailing dimensions to normalize over.  If an
integer `d` is given it is treated as `(d,)`, normalizing
only the last axis.  For a `(N, T, C)` input with
`normalized_shape=(C,)` the mean and variance are computed
independently for each `(n, t)` position.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added to the denominator for numerical stability.
Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: elementwise_affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-element scale $\gamma$ and
(optionally) shift $\beta$ of shape `normalized_shape`.
If `False`, no affine parameters are created and the output is
purely normalized.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description:
Only meaningful when `elementwise_affine=True`.  If `False`,
the module learns only a scale $\gamma$ with no additive
shift.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description:
Device on which to allocate the learnable parameters.
Default: `None` (uses the default device).

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description:
Data type of the learnable parameters.  Default: `None`
(inherits from the input).

examples:
Normalize the last dimension of a sequence model's hidden states:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> ln = nn.LayerNorm(512)
>>> x = lucid.randn(32, 64, 512)   # (batch, seq_len, hidden_dim)
>>> out = ln(x)
>>> out.shape
(32, 64, 512)
Normalize over multiple trailing dimensions (e.g. height and width):
>>> ln2d = nn.LayerNorm((28, 28))
>>> x2d = lucid.randn(8, 1, 28, 28)
>>> out2d = ln2d(x2d)
>>> out2d.shape
(8, 1, 28, 28)
notes:
- Input: $(*, \text{normalized\_shape})$ — any leading batch
  dimensions followed by the normalized trailing dimensions.
- Output: same shape as the input.

- The mean and variance are computed with `correction=0` (biased
  estimator), consistent with the standard layer-norm convention.
- Weights are initialised to 1 and biases to 0 so that the
  transformation is an identity at the start of training.
- When using `elementwise_affine=True, bias=False` the module
  matches the "scale-only" layer norm used in some modern
  architectures.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable per-element scale $\gamma$ of shape
`normalized_shape`.  `None` when
`elementwise_affine=False`.

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
Learnable per-element shift $\beta$ of shape
`normalized_shape`.  `None` when
`elementwise_affine=False` or `bias=False`.

members.name: RMSNorm
members.signature: RMSNorm(normalized_shape: int | list[int] | tuple[int, ...], eps: float = 1e-08, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(normalized_shape: int | list[int] | tuple[int, ...], eps: float = 1e-08, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
methods.summary: Initialise the RMSNorm module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply normalisation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor whose shape is documented in the class docstring.

returns.description: Normalised tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Root Mean Square Layer Normalization.
members.extended:
Normalises the input by its root-mean-square value and applies a
learnable per-element scale, but intentionally omits the mean
subtraction step of standard layer norm:

$$
y = \frac{x}{\mathrm{RMS}(x)} \cdot \gamma, \qquad
\mathrm{RMS}(x) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i^2 + \varepsilon}
$$

where $d$ is the number of elements in `normalized_shape`.

Skipping mean subtraction reduces computation while retaining the
re-scaling invariance property that makes normalization useful.
This formulation is widely used in large language model architectures
such as LLaMA.

parameters.name: normalized_shape
parameters.description:
Shape of the trailing dimensions to normalize over.  An integer
`d` is equivalent to the single-element tuple `(d,)`.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added inside the square root for numerical
stability.  Default: `1e-8`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate `weight`.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type of `weight`.  Default: `None`.

examples:
Typical use in a transformer feed-forward block:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> norm = nn.RMSNorm(256)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 32, 256)   # (batch, seq_len, dim)
>>> out = norm(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 32, 256)
Normalize over two trailing dimensions:
>>> norm2d = nn.RMSNorm((16, 16))
>>> x2d = lucid.randn(2, 8, 16, 16)
>>> out2d = norm2d(x2d)
>>> out2d.shape
(2, 8, 16, 16)
notes:
- Input: $(*, \text{normalized\_shape})$.
- Output: same shape as the input.

- RMSNorm has no bias term; if a shift is needed, add a separate
  bias or use `LayerNorm`.
- The default `eps` (`1e-8`) is intentionally smaller than that
  of `LayerNorm` (`1e-5`), since RMS values can be very
  small for zero-mean inputs.
- The weight is initialised to all ones so the transformation starts
  as a pure normalisation.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable per-element scale $\gamma$ of shape
`normalized_shape`, initialised to ones.  Unlike
`LayerNorm`, RMSNorm has no bias parameter by design.

members.name: GroupNorm
members.signature: GroupNorm(num_groups: int, num_channels: int, eps: float = 1e-05, affine: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_groups: int, num_channels: int, eps: float = 1e-05, affine: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
methods.summary: Initialise the GroupNorm module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply normalisation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor whose shape is documented in the class docstring.

returns.description: Normalised tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Group normalization over the channel dimension.
members.extended:
Divides the $C$ channels into `num_groups` contiguous groups
of size $C / \text{num\_groups}$ and normalises each group
independently over its spatial elements:

$$
y = \frac{x - \mu_g}{\sqrt{\sigma_g^2 + \varepsilon}}
    \cdot \gamma + \beta
$$

where $\mu_g$ and $\sigma_g^2$ are the mean and variance
computed over a single group (channels + spatial axes) for each
sample in the batch.

Group Norm sits between two extremes: `num_groups=1` recovers
Layer Norm (normalize over all channels at once), while
`num_groups=num_channels` recovers Instance Norm (each channel is
its own group).  Unlike Batch Norm, Group Norm statistics are
independent of the batch size, making it stable for small batches
and well-suited to detection and segmentation models.

parameters.name: num_groups
parameters.description:
Number of groups to divide the channels into.
`num_channels` must be divisible by `num_groups`.

parameters.name: num_channels
parameters.description: Total number of channels $C$ expected in the input.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Small constant for numerical stability.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-channel scale $\gamma$ and shift
$\beta$ of shape `(num_channels,)`.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for the learnable parameters.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type of the learnable parameters.  Default: `None`.

examples:
32-channel input split into 8 groups:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> gn = nn.GroupNorm(num_groups=8, num_channels=32)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 32, 64, 64)
>>> out = gn(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 32, 64, 64)
Layer-Norm equivalent (single group) on a 1-D sequence:
>>> gn_layer = nn.GroupNorm(num_groups=1, num_channels=128)
>>> x_seq = lucid.randn(16, 128, 200)   # (N, C, L)
>>> out_seq = gn_layer(x_seq)
>>> out_seq.shape
(16, 128, 200)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, *)$ where $*$ denotes zero or more
  spatial dimensions and $C = \text{num\_channels}$.
- Output: same shape as the input.

- `num_channels` must be divisible by `num_groups`; a
  `ValueError` is raised at the functional level if this is
  violated.
- Despite sharing a name with batch-norm affine parameters, the
  `weight` and `bias` here have shape `(num_channels,)` rather
  than being element-wise over the full normalized region.

attributes.name: weight
attributes.description:
Learnable per-channel scale $\gamma$ of shape
`(num_channels,)`, initialised to ones.
`None` when `affine=False`.

attributes.name: bias
attributes.description:
Learnable per-channel shift $\beta$ of shape
`(num_channels,)`, initialised to zeros.
`None` when `affine=False`.

members.name: BatchNorm1d
members.signature: BatchNorm1d(num_features: int, eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float | None = 0.1, affine: bool = True, track_running_stats: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: Batch normalization over a 2-D or 3-D input `(N, C)` or `(N, C, L)`.
members.extended:
Normalises each channel across the batch (and, for 3-D inputs, the
length) dimension:

$$
y = \frac{x - \mathrm{E}[x]}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}[x] + \varepsilon}}
    \cdot \gamma + \beta
$$

For a 3-D input $(N, C, L)$, the statistics $\mathrm{E}[x]$
and $\mathrm{Var}[x]$ are computed over the $(N, L)$ axes for
each channel $c$.  For a 2-D input $(N, C)$ only the batch
axis $N$ is reduced.

During **training**, batch statistics are used and running statistics
are updated via an exponential moving average:

$$
\hat{\mu} \leftarrow (1 - m)\,\hat{\mu} + m\,\mu_{\text{batch}}
$$

During **evaluation** (`model.eval()`), the stored
`running_mean` and `running_var` are used instead, making
inference independent of batch composition.

parameters.name: num_features
parameters.description: Number of channels $C$.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added to the variance for numerical stability.
Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
Exponential moving average factor for running statistics.
`None` uses a cumulative moving average.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-channel scale $\gamma$ and shift
$\beta$.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, maintains `running_mean`, `running_var`,
and `num_batches_tracked`.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for parameters and buffers.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for parameters and buffers.  Default: `None`.

examples:
2-D input (e.g. a linear layer's activations):
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> bn = nn.BatchNorm1d(128)
>>> x = lucid.randn(32, 128)
>>> out = bn(x)
>>> out.shape
(32, 128)
3-D input — temporal sequence with channels:
>>> bn_seq = nn.BatchNorm1d(64)
>>> x_seq = lucid.randn(16, 64, 200)   # (N, C, L)
>>> out_seq = bn_seq(x_seq)
>>> out_seq.shape
(16, 64, 200)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C)$ or $(N, C, L)$
- Output: same shape as the input.

attributes.name: num_batches_tracked
attributes.description:
Scalar `int64` counting batches seen during training.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

members.name: BatchNorm2d
members.signature: BatchNorm2d(num_features: int, eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float | None = 0.1, affine: bool = True, track_running_stats: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: Batch normalization over a 4-D input `(N, C, H, W)`.
members.extended:
Normalises each channel across the batch and spatial dimensions:

$$
y = \frac{x - \mathrm{E}[x]}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}[x] + \varepsilon}}
    \cdot \gamma + \beta
$$

where $\mathrm{E}[x]$ and $\mathrm{Var}[x]$ are computed
over the $(N, H, W)$ axes for each channel $c$.

During **training**, batch statistics are used and running statistics
are updated via an exponential moving average:

$$
\hat{\mu} \leftarrow (1 - m)\,\hat{\mu} + m\,\mu_{\text{batch}}
$$

During **evaluation** (`model.eval()`), the stored running statistics
`running_mean` and `running_var` are used instead.

parameters.name: num_features
parameters.description: Number of channels $C$.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added to the variance for numerical stability.
Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
Exponential moving average factor for running statistics. If
`None`, uses cumulative moving average. Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-channel scale $\gamma$ and shift
$\beta$. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, maintains `running_mean`, `running_var`,
and `num_batches_tracked`. Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for parameters and buffers.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for parameters and buffers.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(64)
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 64, 32, 32)
>>> out = bn(x)   # normalised per channel
>>> out.shape
(8, 64, 32, 32)
>>> # Eval mode uses running statistics (no batch dependence)
>>> bn.eval()
>>> with lucid.no_grad():
...     out = bn(x)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, H, W)$
- Output: $(N, C, H, W)$ — same shape.

- BatchNorm2d is the most commonly used normalization layer in
  convolutional neural networks.  It stabilises training by keeping
  activations in a well-scaled range after each convolutional block.
- At small batch sizes (e.g. $N < 8$) the batch statistics
  become noisy.  Consider `GroupNorm` or
  `InstanceNorm2d` in those settings.

attributes.name: running_mean
attributes.description:
Running estimate of the per-channel mean, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

attributes.name: running_var
attributes.description:
Running estimate of the per-channel variance, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

attributes.name: num_batches_tracked
attributes.description:
Scalar counting the number of batches seen during training.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

members.name: BatchNorm3d
members.signature: BatchNorm3d(num_features: int, eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float | None = 0.1, affine: bool = True, track_running_stats: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: Batch normalization over a 5-D input `(N, C, D, H, W)`.
members.extended:
Extends batch normalization to volumetric data by normalising each
channel across the batch and all three spatial dimensions:

$$
y = \frac{x - \mathrm{E}[x]}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}[x] + \varepsilon}}
    \cdot \gamma + \beta
$$

where $\mathrm{E}[x]$ and $\mathrm{Var}[x]$ are computed
over the $(N, D, H, W)$ axes for each channel $c$.

The training/evaluation distinction is identical to
`BatchNorm2d` — running statistics are updated during training
and used as fixed normalisation constants during evaluation.

parameters.name: num_features
parameters.description: Number of channels $C$.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Small constant added to the variance for numerical stability.
Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
EMA factor for the running statistics.  `None` selects
cumulative averaging.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-channel $\gamma$ and $\beta$.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, maintains `running_mean`, `running_var`,
and `num_batches_tracked`.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for parameters and buffers.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for parameters and buffers.  Default: `None`.

examples:
Normalizing activations from a 3-D convolution:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> bn3d = nn.BatchNorm3d(32)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 32, 16, 32, 32)   # (N, C, D, H, W)
>>> out = bn3d(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 32, 16, 32, 32)
Disable affine parameters for a parameter-free normaliser:
>>> bn_no_affine = nn.BatchNorm3d(32, affine=False)
>>> out2 = bn_no_affine(x)
>>> out2.shape
(4, 32, 16, 32, 32)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, D, H, W)$
- Output: $(N, C, D, H, W)$ — same shape.

- Typical applications include 3-D convolutional networks for video
  understanding, medical image segmentation (CT/MRI), and any
  domain where the data has a depth axis in addition to height and
  width.
- Because the $(N, D, H, W)$ reduction covers more elements
  than in `BatchNorm2d`, the variance estimate is generally
  more stable at the same batch size.

attributes.name: num_batches_tracked
attributes.description:
Scalar `int64` counting training batches seen.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

members.name: InstanceNorm1d
members.signature: InstanceNorm1d(num_features: int, eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float = 0.1, affine: bool = False, track_running_stats: bool = False, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: Instance normalization for 3-D input `(N, C, L)`.
members.extended:
Normalises each `(n, c)` slice over the length dimension $L$
independently:

$$
y_{n,c} = \frac{x_{n,c} - \mu_{n,c}}
               {\sqrt{\sigma_{n,c}^2 + \varepsilon}}
          \cdot \gamma_c + \beta_c
$$

where $\mu_{n,c}$ and $\sigma_{n,c}^2$ are the mean and
variance of the $L$ elements in sample $n$, channel $c$.

This is useful for 1-D temporal or sequential data where the
statistics should not be mixed across samples or channels.

parameters.name: num_features
parameters.description: Number of channels $C$.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability constant.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description: EMA factor used when `track_running_stats=True`.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description: If `True`, learns per-channel scale and shift.  Default: `False`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, maintains running mean/variance buffers.
Default: `False`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for parameters/buffers.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for parameters/buffers.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> norm = nn.InstanceNorm1d(32)
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 32, 100)   # (N, C, L)
>>> out = norm(x)
>>> out.shape
(8, 32, 100)
With learnable affine parameters:
>>> norm_affine = nn.InstanceNorm1d(32, affine=True)
>>> out2 = norm_affine(x)
>>> out2.shape
(8, 32, 100)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, L)$
- Output: $(N, C, L)$ — same shape.

- With `affine=True`, the learned `weight` and `bias` can be
  replaced at inference time by externally supplied style tensors,
  which is the core mechanism behind adaptive instance normalization
  (AdaIN) used in style transfer.

attributes.name: running_mean
attributes.description:
Running mean per channel, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

attributes.name: running_var
attributes.description:
Running variance per channel, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

members.name: InstanceNorm2d
members.signature: InstanceNorm2d(num_features: int, eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float = 0.1, affine: bool = False, track_running_stats: bool = False, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: Instance normalization for 4-D input `(N, C, H, W)`.
members.extended:
Normalises each `(n, c)` slice over its spatial height and width
independently:

$$
y_{n,c} = \frac{x_{n,c} - \mu_{n,c}}
               {\sqrt{\sigma_{n,c}^2 + \varepsilon}}
          \cdot \gamma_c + \beta_c
$$

where $\mu_{n,c}$ and $\sigma_{n,c}^2$ are computed over
the $H \times W$ spatial positions of sample $n$,
channel $c$.

Unlike `BatchNorm2d`, the statistics do not mix across the
batch, making the output of each image entirely independent of the
other images in the batch.  This property makes InstanceNorm2d
well-suited to image style transfer, generative models, and any
scenario where per-image normalization is desirable.

parameters.name: num_features
parameters.description: Number of feature channels $C$.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability constant.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description: EMA factor for optional running stats.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-channel $\gamma$ and $\beta$.
Default: `False`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, maintains running mean/variance buffers.
Default: `False`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for parameters/buffers.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for parameters/buffers.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> norm = nn.InstanceNorm2d(64)
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 64, 128, 128)
>>> out = norm(x)
>>> out.shape
(4, 64, 128, 128)
With affine parameters for conditional style transfer:
>>> norm_affine = nn.InstanceNorm2d(64, affine=True)
>>> out2 = norm_affine(x)
>>> out2.shape
(4, 64, 128, 128)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, H, W)$
- Output: $(N, C, H, W)$ — same shape.

- Instance Norm was introduced for style transfer, where each image
  should be normalized independently so that stylistic statistics
  from other images do not bleed through.
- At inference time, `weight` and `bias` can be replaced by
  style-conditioned tensors to implement Adaptive Instance
  Normalization (AdaIN).

attributes.name: running_mean
attributes.description:
Running mean per channel, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

attributes.name: running_var
attributes.description:
Running variance per channel, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

members.name: InstanceNorm3d
members.signature: InstanceNorm3d(num_features: int, eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float = 0.1, affine: bool = False, track_running_stats: bool = False, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: Instance normalization for 5-D input `(N, C, D, H, W)`.
members.extended:
Normalises each `(n, c)` slice over the three spatial dimensions
$D \times H \times W$ independently:

$$
y_{n,c} = \frac{x_{n,c} - \mu_{n,c}}
               {\sqrt{\sigma_{n,c}^2 + \varepsilon}}
          \cdot \gamma_c + \beta_c
$$

where $\mu_{n,c}$ and $\sigma_{n,c}^2$ are computed over
the $D \times H \times W$ voxels of sample $n$,
channel $c$.

Applicable to volumetric data such as 3-D medical images or
spatio-temporal video features, where per-sample independence is
important and batch-level statistics are undesirable.

parameters.name: num_features
parameters.description: Number of feature channels $C$.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability constant.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description: EMA factor for optional running stats.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, learns per-channel $\gamma$ and $\beta$.
Default: `False`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, maintains running mean/variance buffers.
Default: `False`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for parameters/buffers.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for parameters/buffers.  Default: `None`.

examples:
3-D volumetric feature maps:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> norm = nn.InstanceNorm3d(16)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 16, 8, 32, 32)   # (N, C, D, H, W)
>>> out = norm(x)
>>> out.shape
(2, 16, 8, 32, 32)
Enable running stats for a test-time normalization baseline:
>>> norm_track = nn.InstanceNorm3d(16, track_running_stats=True)
>>> out2 = norm_track(x)
>>> out2.shape
(2, 16, 8, 32, 32)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, D, H, W)$
- Output: $(N, C, D, H, W)$ — same shape.

attributes.name: running_mean
attributes.description:
Running mean per channel, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

attributes.name: running_var
attributes.description:
Running variance per channel, shape `(num_features,)`.
`None` when `track_running_stats=False`.

members.name: LocalResponseNorm
members.signature: LocalResponseNorm(size: int, alpha: float = 0.0001, beta: float = 0.75, k: float = 1.0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(size: int, alpha: float = 0.0001, beta: float = 0.75, k: float = 1.0)
methods.summary: Initialise the LocalResponseNorm module. See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply normalisation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor whose shape is documented in the class docstring.

returns.description: Normalised tensor of the same shape as `input`.

methods.name: extra_repr
methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Local Response Normalization (LRN) across adjacent channels.
members.extended:
Normalises each activation by a sum of squared activations in a
local neighbourhood of `size` channels centred on the same
spatial position:

$$
y_c = \frac{x_c}{\left(k + \alpha \sum_{j=\max(0,\,c-\lfloor
    \text{size}/2\rfloor)}^{\min(C-1,\,c+\lfloor \text{size}/2\rfloor)}
    x_j^2\right)^{\beta}}
$$

where $C$ is the total number of channels, and the window
$[c - \lfloor \text{size}/2 \rfloor,\; c + \lfloor \text{size}/2 \rfloor]$ is clipped at the channel boundaries.

LRN was introduced in the AlexNet paper as a biologically-inspired
lateral inhibition mechanism: strongly-activated neurons suppress
their neighbors, encouraging competition across feature detectors at
the same spatial location.  It has largely been superseded by Batch
Normalization in modern architectures, but remains available for
reproducing historical results.

parameters.name: size
parameters.description:
Number of adjacent channels included in the normalization
window (the $\text{size}$ term in the formula above).
Must be a positive integer.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Scaling factor $\alpha$ for the squared sum.
Default: `1e-4`.

parameters.name: beta
parameters.description:
Exponent $\beta$ applied to the normalization term.
Default: `0.75`.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description:
Bias constant $k$ that prevents division by zero.
Default: `1.0`.

examples:
Reproduce the AlexNet LRN configuration:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> lrn = nn.LocalResponseNorm(size=5, alpha=1e-4, beta=0.75, k=2.0)
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 96, 27, 27)   # e.g. after first conv in AlexNet
>>> out = lrn(x)
>>> out.shape
(2, 96, 27, 27)
Minimal example with a 1-D channel tensor:
>>> lrn_small = nn.LocalResponseNorm(size=3)
>>> x2 = lucid.randn(4, 32, 64)   # (N, C, L)
>>> out2 = lrn_small(x2)
>>> out2.shape
(4, 32, 64)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, *)$ where $*$ denotes zero or more
  spatial dimensions.  The channel axis must be axis 1.
- Output: same shape as the input.

- The implementation uses a sliding-window sum over the squared
  input, applied via `unfold_dim` on the padded channel axis.
  This avoids an explicit Python loop over channels and is
  fully differentiable through the Lucid engine.
- Inputs with fewer than 2 dimensions are returned unchanged.
- The original AlexNet used `size=5`, `alpha=1e-4`,
  `beta=0.75`, `k=2.0` — adjusting `k` is important to
  control how strongly unsuppressed channels are penalized.

members.name: LazyBatchNorm1d
members.signature: LazyBatchNorm1d(eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float | None = 0.1, affine: bool = True, track_running_stats: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: BatchNorm1d with lazy `num_features` inference.
members.extended:
Identical to `BatchNorm1d` except that `num_features` need
not be specified at construction time.  On the first call to
`forward(x)`, `num_features` is automatically set to
`x.shape[1]` and all parameters and buffers are allocated.

This is convenient when the number of channels is not known at model
definition time, for example when building networks programmatically
or when constructing sub-modules before the input shape is fixed.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability constant.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
EMA factor for running statistics.  `None` uses cumulative
averaging.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, lazily allocates learnable scale and shift.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, lazily allocates running mean/variance and
batch counter.  Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for lazily allocated tensors.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for lazily allocated tensors.  Default: `None`.

examples:
Construct without specifying the channel count:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> bn = nn.LazyBatchNorm1d()
>>> x = lucid.randn(16, 64, 100)   # (N, C, L)
>>> out = bn(x)    # num_features=64 is inferred here
>>> out.shape
(16, 64, 100)
Reload from a checkpoint saved by a BatchNorm1d:
>>> bn2 = nn.LazyBatchNorm1d()
>>> # bn2.load_state_dict(state)  # num_features inferred from saved tensors
notes:
- Input: $(N, C)$ or $(N, C, L)$ — same as
  `BatchNorm1d`.  `C` is inferred on the first forward pass.
- Output: same shape as the input.

members.name: LazyBatchNorm2d
members.signature: LazyBatchNorm2d(eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float | None = 0.1, affine: bool = True, track_running_stats: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: BatchNorm2d with lazy `num_features` inference.
members.extended:
Identical to `BatchNorm2d` except that `num_features` is
inferred from `x.shape[1]` on the first forward pass.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability constant.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
EMA factor for running statistics.  `None` uses cumulative
averaging.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, lazily allocates learnable scale and shift.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, lazily allocates running statistics buffers.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for lazily allocated tensors.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for lazily allocated tensors.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> bn = nn.LazyBatchNorm2d()
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 128, 32, 32)
>>> out = bn(x)    # num_features=128 inferred on first call
>>> out.shape
(8, 128, 32, 32)
Suitable for dynamic architectures where channel count depends on
a configuration value not available at module construction:
>>> def make_norm(affine: bool = True) -> nn.LazyBatchNorm2d:
...     return nn.LazyBatchNorm2d(affine=affine)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, H, W)$ — same as `BatchNorm2d`.
  `C` is inferred on the first forward pass.
- Output: same shape as the input.

members.name: LazyBatchNorm3d
members.signature: LazyBatchNorm3d(eps: float = 1e-05, momentum: float | None = 0.1, affine: bool = True, track_running_stats: bool = True, device: DeviceLike = None, dtype: DTypeLike = None)
members.summary: BatchNorm3d with lazy `num_features` inference.
members.extended:
Identical to `BatchNorm3d` except that `num_features` is
inferred from `x.shape[1]` on the first forward pass.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability constant.  Default: `1e-5`.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description:
EMA factor for running statistics.  `None` uses cumulative
averaging.  Default: `0.1`.

parameters.name: affine
parameters.description:
If `True`, lazily allocates learnable scale and shift.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: track_running_stats
parameters.description:
If `True`, lazily allocates running statistics buffers.
Default: `True`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device for lazily allocated tensors.  Default: `None`.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Data type for lazily allocated tensors.  Default: `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> bn = nn.LazyBatchNorm3d()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 32, 8, 16, 16)   # (N, C, D, H, W)
>>> out = bn(x)    # num_features=32 inferred on first call
>>> out.shape

Useful for 3-D architectures (video, volumetric) where the feature
count is determined by a preceding layer of variable depth:
>>> bn_no_track = nn.LazyBatchNorm3d(track_running_stats=False)
>>> out2 = bn_no_track(x)
>>> out2.shape
(2, 32, 8, 16, 16)
notes:
- Input: $(N, C, D, H, W)$ — same as `BatchNorm3d`.
  `C` is inferred on the first forward pass.
- Output: same shape as the input.

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Apply the pooling operation to the input tensor.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description:
Input tensor of shape $(N, C, *)$ where $*$ are the

returns.description: Pooled output tensor.

methods.summary: Return a string representation of the layer's configuration.

members.summary: Applies 1-D max pooling over a sequence.
members.extended:
For each position in the output, the module selects the maximum value
from a sliding window of length `kernel_size` applied along the last
(length) dimension of the input.

$$
y[n, c, i] = \max_{0 \le k < k_s}
    x\!\left[n,\, c,\, i \cdot s + k \cdot d\right]
$$

where $k_s$ is `kernel_size`, $s$ is `stride`, and
$d$ is `dilation`.

The output length is computed as:

$$
L_{out} = \left\lfloor
    \frac{L_{in} + 2p - d(k_s - 1) - 1}{s} + 1
\right\rfloor
$$

When `ceil_mode=True` the floor is replaced by a ceiling, which may
include one extra partial window at the right boundary.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Size of the sliding window.

parameters.name: stride
parameters.description:
Step between successive windows.  Defaults to `kernel_size` when
`None`.

parameters.name: padding
parameters.description:
Zero-padding added to both ends of the input before pooling.
Default: `0`.

parameters.name: dilation
parameters.description:
Spacing between kernel elements (dilated / atrous pooling).
`dilation=1` gives the standard contiguous window.
Default: `1`.

parameters.name: return_indices
parameters.description:
If `True`, the forward pass also returns the flat indices of
the selected maxima.  Currently not supported (raises
`NotImplementedError`).  Default: `False`.

parameters.name: ceil_mode
parameters.description:
When `True`, use ceiling instead of floor to compute the output
length.  Default: `False`.

examples:
Basic non-overlapping pooling (stride equals kernel_size by default):
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.nn as nn
>>> pool = nn.MaxPool1d(kernel_size=3)
>>> x = lucid.ones((1, 4, 12))
>>> y = pool(x)
>>> y.shape
(1, 4, 4)
Overlapping windows with explicit stride and dilation:
>>> pool = nn.MaxPool1d(kernel_size=3, stride=1, dilation=2)
>>> x = lucid.ones((2, 8, 16))
>>> y = pool(x)
>>> y.shape
(2, 8, 12)
notes:
- Input:  `(N, C, L_in)`
- Output: `(N, C, L_out)`

- Dilation inserts $d - 1$ gaps between consecutive kernel
  elements, effectively enlarging the receptive field without
  increasing the number of comparisons.
- Only the largest value in each window is propagated; gradients flow
  back exclusively to the winning element.
